subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following is the correct statement regarding Huntington's chorea?

options.a: It is a trinucleotide repeat disorder
options.b: There is loss of function type of mutation
options.c: There are abnormal repeats of CUG
options.d: Abnormality is seen due to mutation in chromosome 6

explanation:
Huntington's chorea is a trinucleotide repeat disorder.
It is an autosomal dominant neurodegenerative disorder. It is caused by CAG
trinucleotide repeats (>40). It occurs due to a gain-of-function (expansion) mutation in the
huntingtin gene on chromosome 4p.
The disease shows genetic anticipation and has an early-onset (2-3 decades) if inherited from the
father.
Cognitive impairment is an early feature. Pathologically, atrophy of caudate and putamen with ex
vacuo ventriculomegaly is seen. Decreased acetylcholine leads to dementia and decreased GABA
and increased dopamine lead to chorea.
Treatment consists of haloperidol and tetrabenazine.

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following statements is true about the pathology of achalasia cardia?

options.c: Decreased release of NO and VIP in the distal part of esophagus
options.d: Decreased tone of the lower esophageal sphincter

explanation:
Achalasia cardia is characterized by decreased release of NO(nitric oxide) and VIP(vasoactive
intestinal polypeptide) in the distal part of esophagus.
Normally, NO and VIP are secreted by the inhibitory neurons of the Auerbach's plexus in the
distal esophagus. This leads to relaxation of the lower esophageal sphincter (LES). In achalasia
cardia, these inhibitory neurons degenerate leading to decreased release of NO and VIP. This
causes increased LES tone.
The triad of achalasia cardia is as follows:
• Incomplete relaxation of LES
• Increased tone of LES
• Absent peristalsis (aperistalisis)

subject: Pathology
question:
A patient presents with midline swelling in the neck. Biopsy revealing Orphan Annie eye
nuclei is shown in the image below. What is the diagnosis?

[Pathology-NEET-2020-3-image]
1966
explanation:
The presence of empty appearing nuclei, also called Orphan Annie eye nuclei as seen in the above
image is diagnostic of papillary carcinoma of the thyroid.
Microscopic features of papillary thyroid carcinoma include:
• Branching papillae with fibrovascular stalk
• Covering epithelium is simple or stratified cuboidal
• Orphan Annie eye nuclei -
• Empty-appearing nuclei due to dispersed chromatin
• They are diagnostic, regardless of papillary architecture
• Psammoma bodies - concentric calcified structures
• Foci of lymphatic invasion
The image below shows papillary thyroid carcinoma with characteristic Orphan Annie eye nuclei:
1978

[Pathology-NEET-2020-3-exp-image]

[Pathology-NEET-2020-3-exp-image]

[Pathology-NEET-2020-3-exp-image]
1979
Note: Orphan Annie refers to a comic book character with empty appearing eyes.

subject: Pathology
question:
A 51-year-old male patient came with complaints of hematuria to the clinic. On examination,
he was normotensive and had pedal edema. Investigations revealed the patient had no
glycosuria and had a creatinine of 9. His renal biopsy is shown below. Which of the following
investigations should be done to identify the disease?

[Pathology-NEET-2020-4-image]
explanation:
Based on the given clinical history and microscopy showing crescents, rapidly progressive
glomerulonephritis (RPGN) is suspected. Among the given options, anti- GBM antibody
testing should be done, since it is a part of the work-up of RPGN.

[Pathology-NEET-2020-4-exp-image]

[Pathology-NEET-2020-4-exp-image]
Type 1 RPGN includes renal limited disease and Goodpasture's disease. In this condition,
antibodies are produced against the non-collagenous domain of the α3 chain of collagen IV of the
GBM.
Microscopy findings of Goodpasture's disease are:
1980
• Immunofluorescence - linear deposits of Ig and complement along the glomerular basement
membrane
• Electron microscopy - wrinkling of the glomerular basement membrane with focal
disruptions without any immune complex deposition.
A variant of Goodpasture's disease with pulmonary involvement is known as Goodpasture's
syndrome.
Option A: ANA testing is done if SLE is suspected. Electron microscopy will show immune
complex deposits.
Option C: HIV RNA testing is done in immunodeficiency syndrome associated with weight loss.
Electron microscopy will show diffuse podocyte injury with complete foot process effacement.
Tubuloreticular inclusions will be seen in endothelial cells.
Option D: Urine electrophoresis is done if nephrotic syndrome is suspected.

subject: Pathology
question:
A 50-year-old man presented with a retroperitoneal mass. Biopsy from the lesion is shown in
the image below. Molecular analysis revealed t(12;16). What is the most likely condition?
1967

[Pathology-NEET-2020-5-image]
explanation:
The given clinical scenario is suggestive of myxoid liposarcoma.
The image shows abundant ground substance, with branching capillaries and a few immature
adipocytes.
Liposarcoma is the most common sarcoma in adults and is usually seen in the 5th to 6th decade
of life. They commonly occur in the deep soft tissues of the proximal extremities and in the
retroperitoneum.
Histologically the liposarcomas are divided into three subtypes:
• Well-differentiated liposarcoma: It consists of mature adipocytes and scattered spindle cells with
hyperchromatic nuclei. It is associated with amplification of 12q13-q15.
• Myxoid liposarcoma: It contains abundant basophilic extracellular matrix with branching
capillaries. Few immature adipocytes are found scattered within the matrix. It is associated with
t(12;16).
• Pleomorphic liposarcoma: It consists of sheets of anaplastic cells with bizarre nuclei and few
immature adipocytes.
Among the three subtypes, the pleomorphic liposarcoma is the most malignant and often
metastasizes, while the well-differentiated is least malignant. Myxoid liposarcoma has
intermediate malignancy behaviour.

subject: Pathology
question: Large, friable, irregular vegetations on heart valves are seen in which condition?
explanation:
Large, friable, irregular vegetations on heart valves are seen in infective endocarditis.
Infective endocarditis is characterized by the presence of large, friable, irregular vegetations on
heart valves. Aortic and mitral valves are the most commonly affected, however, in some cases,
the right heart valves may be involved.
1981
Vegetations may be single or multiple and may involve one or more valves. Embolization often
occurs and may lead to septic infarcts or mycotic aneurysms at the site of lodgement due to the
presence of virulent organisms in the emboli.

subject: Pathology
question:
A 23-year-old male patient presented with a history of fatigue and tiredness. On investigation,
he was found to have Hb = 9 gm% and MCV =101 FL. Peripheral smear examination showed
macrocytic RBC and hypersegmented neutrophils. Which of the following is the most likely
etiology?
explanation:
The given findings are suggestive of macrocytic anaemia, which can be seen in chronic alcoholics.
Anemic patients with a peripheral smear showing macrocytic RBCs and hypersegmented
neutrophils are important characteristic features of megaloblastic anemia.
Chronic alcoholics also have deficiency of folic acid due to:
• Trapping of folate within the liver
• Excessive urinary loss
• Disordered folate metabolism
• Inadequate dietary intake is frequently encountered in chronic alcoholics

subject: Pathology
question:
A 5-year-old child was admitted to the hospital with a history of prolapsing rectal mass and
painless rectal bleeding. The histopathological image of the mass is as shown below. What is
the most likely diagnosis?

[Pathology-NEET-2020-8-image]
explanation:
The clinical features and the histopathological image showing characteristic cystically dilated
crypts and glands are suggestive of a juvenile polyp.

[Pathology-NEET-2020-8-exp-image]
1982

[Pathology-NEET-2020-8-exp-image]
Juvenile polyps are malformations of epithelium and lamina propria commonly located in the
rectum. It is seen in children <5 years of age. The typical presentation is painless rectal
bleeding. Sometimes there may be a proliferating rectal mass.
The disease may be sporadic or syndromic.
• Sporadic juvenile polyp occurs as a single mass and is called retention polyp.
• Juvenile polyposis syndrome is characterized by multiple hamartomatous polyps, which may be
premalignant.
Morphologically, both of these types are indistinguishable.
• Gross appearance: They appear as pedunculated, smooth reddish lesions, and with cystic spaces.
• Microscopic appearance: The cysts appear as dilated glands filled with mucin and inflammatory
debris.
Note: Complex glandular architecture and the presence of smooth muscle cutting through the
lamina propria are seen in Peutz-Jeghers polyps.

subject: Pathology
question:
A 20-year-old patient presented to the clinic with a history of malabsorption, diarrhea, and
poor appetite. Duodenal biopsy of the patient showed crypt hyperplasia, villi atrophy, and
CD8+ cells in lamina propria. What is the most probable diagnosis?
explanation:
The clinical presentation and the histopathological findings are suggestive of celiac disease.
Celiac disease is an autoimmune gluten-induced enteropathy where HLA DQ2 and DQ8 are
expressed. In the small intestine, peptides of gluten are presented to helper T cells which mediate
mucosal inflammation. Antiendomysial and anti-tTG antibodies are found in serological studies.
Clinically, malabsorption syndrome with diarrhea, abdominal pain, and marked weight loss is
seen along with chronic refractory anemia. Dermatitis herpetiformis is seen in 10% of patients
with celiac disease. Microscopic findings show villi atrophy, villi flattening, crypt hyperplasia, and
increased intraepithelial T lymphocytes (IEL).
1983
Option A: Environmental enteropathy / tropical sprue is seen in tropical areas with poor
sanitation. It is believed to be caused by one or more infectious agents that respond to antibiotics.
Mucosa shows variable villous atrophy.
Option C: Whipple's disease is a multisystemic disease caused by T.whipplei. Small intestinal
biopsy shows PAS-positive macrophages.

subject: Pathology
question:
A 3-year-old child was brought to the hospital with complaints of fever, rash and swelling of
her hands and feet since past 5 days. There is desquamative skin rash, oedema of hands and
feet, erythema of palms and soles, oral erythema, and cervical lymphadenopathy. What is the
likely condition?
explanation:
The given clinical scenario is suggestive of Kawasaki disease or mucocutaneous lymph node
syndrome.
Kawasaki disease is an acute febrile illness associated with arteritis of medium to large-sized
vessels. It is commonly seen in infants and children <4 years of age.
Clinical features include:
• Fever
• Conjunctival and oral erythema
• Blistering, edema of hands and feet
• Erythema of palms and soles
• Desquamative rash
• Cervical lymphadenopathy
The disease is usually self-limiting. In some cases, the disease may involve coronary artery leading
to thrombosis and acute myocardial infarction. Intravenous immunoglobulin and aspirin may be
given to reduce the risk of coronary artery disease.

subject: Pathology
question: Effacement of podocyte foot processes is seen in which of the following diseases?
explanation:
Effacement of the podocyte foot processes is characteristically seen in minimal change disease
(MCD), which is also called lipoid nephrosis.
It is also seen in other conditions such as membranous nephropathy and focal segmental
glomerulosclerosis. Since the podocytes are affected in these diseases, they are also known as
podocytopathies.
The effacement of foot process can only be observed on electron microscopy.

subject: Pathology
question: Thrombosis is initiated by:
explanation:
Thrombosis is initiated by endothelial injury.
Thrombosis is is the formation of a blood clot inside a blood vessel, obstructing the flow of blood
through the circulatory system.
1984
Three primary abnormalities, often referred to as Virchow triad, are known to induce thrombosis.
They include:
• Endothelial injury - most important factor
• Stasis or turbulent blood flow
• Hypercoagulability of the blood

subject: Pathology
question:
A middle-aged woman presents with thyroid swelling. Blood tests show elevated TSH levels
and the presence of thyroid peroxidase antibodies. Histopathological examination revealed
the presence of Hurthle Icells. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
This clinical scenario with the presence of Hurthle cells is suggestive of Hashimoto's thyroiditis.
It is an autoimmune disease, characterized by the gradual destruction of the thyroid gland leading
to thyroid failure. It is commonly seen in women between 45 to 65 years of age. It is caused by the
presence of circulating autoantibodies against thyroglobulin and thyroid peroxidase.
Morphologically, there is a diffuse enlargement of the thyroid gland, with an intact capsule. It is
well demarcated from the adjacent structures.
Microscopic features include:
• Intense lymphocytic infiltrate containing small lymphocytes, plasma cells, and well developed
germinal centers
• Atrophied thyroid follicles
• The epithelial cells lining the follicles contain eosinophilic granular cytoplasm
• This is called oncocytic metaplasia
• These cells are called Hurthle cells

[Pathology-NEET-2020-13-exp-image]
1985

[Pathology-NEET-2020-13-exp-image]

subject: Pathology
question:
A 52-year-old male patient comes with complaints of abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting.
A peripheral blood smear reveals macrocytes and hypersegmented neutrophils and an
endoscopy reveals atrophic gastritis. Which of the following conditions is likely to be seen this
patient?
explanation:
The given findings are suggestive of pernicious anemia, which is associated with atrophic gastritis
and vitamin B12 deficiency.
Anemic patient with a peripheral smear showing macrocytic RBCs and hypersegmented
neutrophils are important characteristic features of megaloblastic anemia. Vitamin B12 and folic
acid deficiency are the main causes of megaloblastic anemia.
Pernicious anemia is an autoimmune disease characterized by the presence of autoantibodies
which impairs the production or activity of the intrinsic factor (IF). This is required for the uptake
of vitamin B12. There are three types of autoantibodies namely, type I, II, and III. The action of
each of these include:
• Autoantibody type I prevents the binding of vitamin B12 to the intinsic factor (IF).
• Autoantibody type II prevents the binding of vitamin B12-IF complex to the ileal receptors.
• Autoantibody type III acts against the α and β subunits of the gastric proton pump, which
impairs the production of intrinsic factor.
These patients also develop chronic atrophic gastritis, characterized by loss of parietal cells,
infiltration of lymphocytes and plasma cells and megaloblastic changes in mucosal cells.

subject: Pathology
question:
A 35-year-old woman presents with a lump in the upper outer quadrant of her breast.
Histopathological examination of the mass showed cells in mucin pools and faint nuclei.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
The given scenario is suggestive of colloid or mucinous carcinoma of the breast.
Colloid carcinoma is an invasive breast carcinoma, which shows intracellular and extracellular
mucin deposition.
1986
On histopathological examination, extracellular type shows tumor cells with faint nuclei, arranged
in clusters, floating in lakes or pools of mucin. In intracellular type, nuclei are pushed to the
periphery to form a signet ring appearance.

[Pathology-NEET-2020-15-exp-image]

[Pathology-NEET-2020-15-exp-image]
Option B: Microscopic image of papillary carcinoma of the breast showing true papillae with a
leaf-like arrangement of fibrovascular tissue lined by the tumor cells is shown below.
1987

[Pathology-NEET-2020-15-exp-image]

[Pathology-NEET-2020-15-exp-image]
Option C: Microscopic image of medullary carcinoma of the breast consisting of sheets of highly
pleomorphic cells with mitotic figures (anaplastic cells), lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate is shown
below.
1988

[Pathology-NEET-2020-15-exp-image]
Option D: Microscopic image of lobular carcinoma of the breast shows infiltrating small
monomorphic discohesive tumor cells arranged in an Indian-file or single-file pattern is shown
below.

[Pathology-NEET-2020-15-exp-image]

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following is true about the pathogenesis of Hirschsprung disease?

options.a: Failure of migration of neural crest cells

explanation:
Hirschsprung disease occurs due to the failure of migration of the neural crest cells to the bowel
wall.
During normal embryogenesis, the neural crest cells migrate from the caecum to the rectum. The
enteric neuronal plexuses develop from these migrated neural crest cells.
In Hirschsprung disease, there is an arrest in the migration of these cells, which leads to the
development of a distal intestinal segment that is devoid of Meissner (submucosal) and the
1989
Auerbach (myenteric) plexus. Hence this segment is referred to as the aganglionic segment.
As a result, there is a lack of coordination in the peristaltic contractions and functional
obstruction, which further leads to the dilation of the intestine proximal to the affected segment,
leading to congenital megacolon. Diagnosis is confirmed by demonstrating the absence of
ganglion cells in biopsy sections.

[Pathology-NEET-2020-16-exp-image]
Some conditions may cause acquired megacolon. These include:
• Chagas disease, due to the loss of ganglion cells
• Visceral myopathy
• Obstruction by neoplasm or inflammatory stricture
• Toxic megacolon complicating ulcerative colitis
• Functional psychosomatic disorders

subject: Pathology
question:
1971
Premature aging in Werner syndrome is due to:

options.c: Increase in length of telomere due to decrease in telomerase activity

explanation:
Premature aging in Werner syndrome is due to a defect in DNA helicase.
DNA helicase is involved in DNA replication and repair and other functions requiring DNA
unwinding. A defect in this enzyme causes rapid accumulation of damaged chromosomes that
may mimic the injury that normally accumulates during cellular aging.

subject: Pathology
question:
A 24-year-old presented with swelling in the wrist joint. Histopathological examination of the
swelling revealed spindle cells with palisading of the nuclei and Verocay bodies. Which of the
following is the most likely condition?
explanation:
The histopathological examination is suggestive of schwannoma. Features showing spindle cells
with palisading nuclei and Verocay bodies are characteristic of schwannoma.
1990
Schwannomas are well-circumscribed, encapsulated masses that abut the associated nerve
without invading it. They are commonly associated with inactivating mutations in the NF2 gene
on chromosome 22q12.
Microscopically, it consists of dense areas called Antoni A, and loose areas called Antoni B.
• The Antoni A areas contain spindle cells that are arranged into intersecting fascicles. Palisading
of nuclei is seen, with nuclear-free zones between them, forming structures called Verocay bodies.
• The Antoni B areas contain spindle-shaped cells that are loosely arranged separated by
prominent myxoid extracellular matrix.
The image below shows cellular Antoni A and paucicellullar Antoni B areas in schwannoma.

[Pathology-NEET-2020-18-exp-image]
The image below shows verocay bodies in the Antoni-A area of vestibular schwannoma.

[Pathology-NEET-2020-18-exp-image]
1991

subject: Pathology
question: B-cells are induced to produce IgE by which of the following:
explanation: IL-4 stimulates B-cells to differentiate into IgE secreting plasma cells.

question:

shows a change from columnar to stratified squamous epithelium, as depicted in the image
below. The change is called:
1972

explanation:

subject: Pathology
question: Graft from identical twin is called:
explanation:
A graft from an identical twin is called an isograft.
Isograft is defined as an organ or tissue that is taken from a donor and is transplanted to a
recipient who has the same genetic constitution as the donor. For example, graft between identical
twins.
1992

subject: Pathology
question: Histology of reperfused myocardium:
explanation:
Reperfused myocardium shows eosinophilic contraction bands on light microscopy. They occur
in irreversibly injured myocytes with damaged membranes, when blood supply is restored 12-24
hours after MI.
During ischemia, the intracellular levels of calcium are increased due to impaired calcium cycling
and sarcolemmal damage. This leads to augmented and uncontrolled contraction of myofibrils
after reperfusion.
On microscopic examination, the irreversibly injured myocytes exhibit contraction bands that are
intensely eosinophilic intracellular stripes. These contraction bands are composed of closely
packed sarcomeres.
Reperfused infarcts are usually hemorrhagic because the vasculature is injured during ischemia.
This leads to bleeding after the flow is restored.

subject: Pathology
question:
All-trans retinoic acid is used in the treatment of tumor that is associated with which of the
following genetic aberrations?
explanation:
All-trans retinoic acid (ATRA) is used in the treatment of acute promyelocytic leukemia (APML),
which is associated with PML-RARA fusion gene.
Acute promyelocytic leukemia (APML) is associated with a reciprocal translocation between
chromosomes 15 and 17 producing a PML-RARA fusion gene. This fusion gene encodes for a
chimeric protein, which consists of a part of the PML protein and a part of the retinoic acid
receptor-α (RARα).
The normal RARα binds to the DNA and activates the transcription in the presence of retinoids.
However, the chimeric protein produced by PML-RARA has reduced affinity for retinoids. This
causes inhibition of transcription, thereby preventing gene expression for cellular differentiation.
This leads to the accumulation of myeloid progenitor cells.
The condition is treated with all-trans retinoic acid. It binds to the PML-RARA chimeric protein.
This causes conformational change such that the protein regains its normal function and activates
DNA transcription.

subject: Pathology
question:
A 12-year-old child presented with 15 days of cough. On examination, cervical
lymphadenopathy was noted and the lymph node biopsy showed the following features. What
is the most probable diagnosis?

[Pathology-NEET-2020-24-image]
explanation:
The histopathological picture showing the caseating granuloma, eosinophilic necrotic debris
known as caseation, bordered by collections of epithelioid cells, Langhan giant cells (horse shoe
shape) and lymphocytes and clinical history is suggestive of tuberculosis.
Clinically, tuberculosis presents with chronic cough, fever, weight loss, swollen lymph nodes.
Other options:
Option B- Leprosy presents with skin lesions and nerve involvement with loss of sensation.
Option C- Sarcoidosis shows giant cells with cytoplasmic inclusions, non-caseating granuloma
with fibroblastic proliferation at the periphery.
1993
Option D- Syphilis shows thickened capsule/ hyperplasia of capsule accompanied by fibrosis,
perivascular inflammation, and infiltration of chronic inflammatory cells.
The below image shows histopathological findings seen in tuberculosis

[Pathology-NEET-2020-24-exp-image]

subject: Pathology
question:
A 30-year-old woman with a long history of dyspnea, chronic cough with sputum, and
wheezing died of respiratory failure following a bout of lobar pneumonia. She was not a
1974
smoker or an alcoholic. The lung at autopsy is shown in the image below. Which of the
following is most likely to cause the pathological changes shown?

[Pathology-NEET-2020-25-image]
explanation:
The above gross specimen shows emphysematous damage involving enlargement of the entire
acinus suggestive of panacinar emphysema. It is caused due to alpha-1-antitrypsin deficiency.
In panacinar (panlobular) emphysema, the acini are uniformly enlarged from the level of the
respiratory bronchiole to the terminal blind alveoli. It occurs commonly in the lower zones and in
the anterior margins of the lung and is most severe at the bases.
The image below shows panacinar emphysema.

[Pathology-NEET-2020-25-exp-image]
1994

subject: Pathology
question:
A 6-foot-5-inch man undergoing a routine physical examination was found to have an early
diastolic murmur. His family pedigree chart is given below. Identify the mode of inheritance
of this condition.
1975

[Pathology-NEET-2020-26-image]
explanation:
The patient's height and the presence of early diastolic murmur in the given clinical scenario are
suggestive of Marfan syndrome. It is inherited in an autosomal dominant disorder. The family
pedigree confirms the mode of inheritance.
The following flow chart helps in making out the pattern of inheritance from a given pedigree
chart.

[Pathology-NEET-2020-26-exp-image]
Patients with Marfan syndrome have pleiotropic manifestations:
• Skeletal systems - Tall and decreased upper to lower segment ratio with long arms,
hands, and fingers
• Deformities of the chest wall, vertebral column, and foot arches
• Cardiovascular abnormalities - mitral valve prolapse leading to mitral
regurgitation and dilatation of aortic root leading to aortic regurgitation, aortic dissection, and
rupture.
• Ocular - superio-temporal lens dislocation
1995

subject: Pathology
question: What is the cell of origin of Chronic Lymphocytic Leukaemia/Small Lymphocytic Lymphoma?
explanation:
The cell of origin of Chronic Lymphocytic Leukaemia (CLL)/ Small Lymphocytic Lymphoma (SLL)
is either naive B cells or post-germinal centre memory B cell.
The CLL and SLL differ only in the degree of peripheral blood lymphocytes. CLL is the most
common leukemia of adults in the western world. The median age of diagnosis is 60 years. The
most common genetic anomalies seen are deletions of 13q14.3, 11q, and 17p and trisomy 12q. Loss
of tumor suppressor genes in chromosome 13 results in overexpression of the anti-apoptotic
protein BCL2.
The growth of CLL/SLL cells is confined to the proliferation centers (pathognomonic for
CLL/SLL), where the tumor cells receive critical factors from the surrounding environments, such
as NF-κB for cell survival, MYC for cell growth, Bruton Tyrosine Kinase (BTK) signaling pathway,
etc.,
The tumor cells express CD19, CD20, CD23, and CD5. Smudge cells are also characteristic of
CLL. Clinical features include easy fatiguability, weight loss, anorexia, generalized
lymphadenopathy, hepatomegaly, hemolytic anemia, etc. CLL/SLL has a tendency to transform to
a more aggressive diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL)  - this is called Richter syndrome.
Factors with a bad prognosis include:
• Presence of deletions of 11q and  17p.
• Lack of somatic hypermutation.
• Expression of ZAP-70, a protein that augments signals produced by the Ig receptor.
• Presence of NOTCH1 mutations
BTK inhibitors and BCL2 inhibitors produce sustained therapeutic responses in a higher fractions
of people with CLL/SLL.

subject: Pathology
question: Human Papilloma Virus inactivates RB gene by which of the following proteins?
explanation:
E7 protein of Human Papilloma Virus (HPV) binds to the hypophosphorylated RB protein and
displaces the E2F transcription factors, thereby promoting G1/S transition.
RB gene is a key negative regulator of the cell cycle at the G1/S transition. RB gene exists in an
active hypophosphorylated state in resting cells and in an inactive hyperphosphorylated state in
cells undergoing G1/S transition.
E7 protein from the high-risk types of HPV (such as HPV 16) binds to the hypophosphorylated RB
through the same pocket that RB uses to bind and sequester the E2F transcription factors, thereby
2011
displacing the E2F which leads to the progression of the cell cycle and thus the proliferation of
tumor cells. E7 proteins also inactivate the CDK inhibitors p21 and p27. E7 proteins from
high-risk HPVs (types 16,18 and 31) also bind and presumably activates cyclins  A and E.
Oncogenic activities of protein E6:
The E6 protein binds to and helps in the degradation of p53 and stimulates the expression of
telomerase reverse transcriptase (TERT), which is the catalytic subunit of telomerase, thereby
helping in the longevity of the cells. E6 from high-risk HPV types has a higher affinity for p53
than E6 from low-risk types.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-2-exp-image]

subject: Pathology
question:
In cystic fibrosis, which of the following structure is affected in pancreas?

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-3-image]
1996
explanation:
The part labeled ‘B’ in the image is the exocrine pancreas which is affected in cystic fibrosis.
Features of cystic fibrosis in the pancreas:
• Accumulation of mucus in the ducts leads to dilation of exocrine glands.
• Complete blockage of ducts with mucus leads to atrophy of the exocrine pancreas and
progressive fibrosis
• Due to atrophy, there will be loss of exocrine secretions, which in turn leads to impaired
absorption of fat and fat-soluble vitamins
• Associated loss of vitamin A leads to squamous metaplasia of the pancreatic duct lining
epithelium
2012

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-3-exp-image]
Other options:
A- Endocrine pancreas
C- Pancreatic ducts
D- Blood vessels

subject: Pathology
question: Mediators involved in the early phase of type 1 hypersensitivity are?
explanation:
The mediators involved in the early phase of type 1 hypersensitivity are,
• Mast cells,
• Histamine,
• Prostaglandins (D2) and
• Leukotrienes (C4 and D4).
Early phase of type 1 hypersensitivity:
The mediators produced by the mast cells are responsible for most of the immediate
hypersensitivity reactions. Histamine and leukotrienes are released rapidly from the sensitized
mast cells and trigger intense immediate reactions characterized by edema, mucus secretion and
smooth muscle spasm.
Other mediators involved in the early phase are,
• Enzymes: neutral proteases (chymase, tryptase) and acid hydrolases
• Proteoglycans: heparin and chondroitin sulfate
• Lipid mediators: Platelet-activating factor (PAF)
Late-phase of type 1 hypersensitivity:
2013
The late phase reaction is characterized by the recruitment of leukocytes, that amplify and sustain
the inflammatory response without exposure to the triggering antigen.
Mediators involved in the late phase of type 1 hypersensitivity reaction are:
• Cytokines: TNF, IL-1, IL-4, IL-5, and chemokines (eotaxin)
• Leukocytes (eosinophils)
• Major Basic Protein (MBP)
• Eosinophil cationic protein

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-4-exp-image]

subject: Pathology
question:
Given below is an image of activation of the coagulation cascade, following closed endothelial
injury. Identify the factor ‘L’ which combines with tissue factor to activate factor IX.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-5-image]
explanation:
The factor marked as ‘L’ is factor VII, which combines with tissue factor that is produced
following a close endothelial injury to activate factor IX.
As the image represents the in vivo coagulation cascade, the tissue factor is the major initiator of
coagulation. It is believed that in vivo, factor VIIa/tissue factor complex is the most important
activator of factor IX and that factor IXa/factor VIIIa complex is the most important activator of
factor X.
Tissue factor is expressed on the surfaces of the subendothelial cellular components of the vessel
wall, such as smooth muscle cells and fibroblasts. This tissue factor is exposed to blood when
there is a vascular damage. This activates the in vivo extrinsic pathway of coagulation cascade,
which is as follows:
• Tissue factor binds with serine protease factor VIIa
• This complex then activates factor IX to factor IXa
• Factor IXa then activates factor X to factor Xa
• Factor Xa can be formed through the actions of either the tissue factor-factor VIIa complex or
factor IXa (with factor VIIIa as cofactor)
2014
• Factor Xa then converts prothrombin to thrombin, which is the pivotal protease of the
coagulation cascade.
• The cofactor essential for this conversion is factor Va.
• The thrombin then formed converts the soluble plasma fibrinogen to an insoluble fibrin matrix.
• Thrombin also activates factor XIII, which is the fibrin stabilizing factor, to factor XIIIa. This
factor XIIIa forms covalent cross-links and thereby stabilizes the fibrin clot.
The below image shows the in vivo coagulation cascade.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-5-exp-image]

The tubes are inverted five times to initiate the clotting process.
Note: Red stopper glass tubes are often referred to as clot or plain tubes because they contain no
anticoagulants or additives.

subject: Pathology
question: Arrange the following cellular events of inflammation in the correct sequence:
explanation:
The correct sequence of cellular events that occurs during inflammation is as follows: Rolling,
Cytokine-mediated integrin activation, Adhesion, Migration.
The recruitment of leucocytes to the site of inflammation is a multi-step process that is mediated
by adhesion molecules and cytokines called chemokines. This process can be divided into
sequential phases:
• In the lumen: margination, rolling and adhesion to the endothelium occurs.
• Migration of the leucocytes across the endothelium and vessel wall
• Migration in the tissues towards a chemotactic stimulus.
The process of leucocyte recruitment is as follows:
• Margination: Because of the dilation of the inflamed postcapillary venues, there is stasis of blood
flow, and hence, more leucocytes assume a peripheral position along the endothelial surface; this
process of leucocyte redistribution is called margination.
• Rolling: The slowed down leucocytes then sense the signals from the endothelium and start
rolling on the vessel wall.
• Cytokine-mediated integrin activation: The attachment of leucocytes to the endothelial cells
requires adhesion molecules that are activated by cytokines. The major families of proteins
involved in adhesion and migration are selectins and integrins and their ligands.
• Adhesions: The weak rolling interactions slow down the leucocytes and allow them to bind more
firmly to the endothelium. Chemokines that were produced at the site of injury bind to the
endothelial cell proteoglycans. These chemokines then bind to the rolling leucocytes and activate
them. This, in turn, converts the ligands for β1 and β2 integrins, VLA-4, and LFA-1 present on the
leucocytes to a high-affinity state.
• This combination of cytokine-induced expression of integrin ligands on the endothelium and
increased integrin affinity on the leucocytes leads to firm adhesion of leucocytes to the
endothelium at the site of inflammation.
• Leucocyte migration: This is the movement of leucocytes through the intact endothelium, called
transmigration or diapedesis. This occurs in postcapillary venules. This migration occurs through
the inter-endothelial gap towards the chemical concentration gradient created by the chemokines
at the site of injury. This occurs with the help of adhesion molecules present in the intercellular
junctions such as platelet endothelial cell adhesion molecule (PECAM-1) or CD31.
2016

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-7-exp-image]

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following is used in the treatment of sideroblastic anemia?
explanation:
Vitamin B6 (Pyridoxine) is used in the treatment of sideroblastic anaemia.
Pyridoxine 200mg per day for 2-3 months is administered routinely to all cases of sideroblastic
anaemia. This cofactor is essential for ALA-synthase activity, and mutations in the pyridoxine
binding site of the enzyme have been found in several responsive patients. Blood transfusion and
other supportive therapy are also indicated. In cases of secondary sideroblastic anaemia, the
primary focus is the removal of the offending agent. But no definitive treatment is available for
hereditary and idiopathic types of sideroblastic anaemia.
Sideroblasts are nucleated red cells (normoblasts) containing siderotic granules which stain
positively with Prussian blue stain. Based on aetiology, sideroblastic anaemia are classified into
hereditary and acquired types.
Hereditary sideroblastic anaemia is an X-linked disorder caused due to a defect in the activity of
aminolevulinic acid (ALA) synthetase required for heme synthesis. Affected females act as carriers
while males have moderate to marked anaemia and manifest in childhood or during early
adulthood
Acquired sideroblastic anaemia:
I) Primary acquired:
• Also called idiopathic or refractory acquired sideroblastic anaemia
• Occurs in middle-aged and older individuals of both sexes
• Due to the reduced activity of the enzyme ALA synthetase, there is disturbed growth and
maturation of the erythroid precursors at the level of hematopoietic stem cell
• Moderate to severe anaemia, which appears insidiously
• Neutropenia and thrombocytopenia may also be seen
2017
• Regarded as a myelodysplastic disorder in the FAB classification and thus considered
preleukaemic
II) Secondary acquired:
May appear secondary to
• Drugs, chemicals and toxins: Isoniazid, Cycloserine, Chloramphenicol, Cyclophosphamide,
Alcohol, Lead, etc.,
• Haematological disorders: myelofibrosis, polycythemia vera, acute leukaemia, myeloma,
lymphoma and hemolytic anaemia.
• Miscellaneous: Carcinoma, myxoedema, rheumatoid arthritis and SLE.
Laboratory findings:
• Peripheral smear: Hypochromic, microcytic (sometimes normocytic) anaemia
• Absolute values: MCV, MCH and MCHC are reduced except in acquired type, where MCV is
raised.
• Bone marrow: Erythroid hyperplasia with macronormoblastic erythropoiesis. Marrow iron stores
are raised. Pathognomonic ring sideroblasts are present.
• Serum ferritin: Raised
• Serum iron: Raised
• TIBC: Normal
• Haemoglobin electrophoresis: Normal

subject: Pathology
question:
A 5-year-old child who presented with proptosis of one of the eyes was found to have a
desmin positive tumour. What is the probable diagnosis?
explanation:
The clinical picture of a 5-year-old child with proptosis and a desmin positive tumor suggests a
diagnosis of embryonal rhabdomyosarcoma.
Rhabdomyosarcoma is a malignant mesenchymal tumor with skeletal muscle differentiation.
There are four subtypes: alveolar, embryonal, pleomorphic, and spindle
cell/sclerosing. Rhabdomyosarcoma is the most common soft tissue sarcoma of childhood and
adolescence and is usually of the alveolar and the embryonal variety. Pleomorphic
rhabdomyosarcoma is seen predominantly in adults. Spindle cell/sclerosing type affects all ages.
The pediatric forms often arise in the sinuses, head and neck, and genitourinary tract.
Embryonal rhabdomyosarcoma has sheets of both primitive round and spindled cells in a myxoid
stroma. Rhabdomyoblasts with visible cross-striations may be present. Sarcoma botryoids is
a variant of embryonal rhabdomyosarcoma in the walls of hollow, mucosal-lined structures, such
as the nasopharynx, common bile duct, bladder, and vagina with cambium layer. Alveolar
rhabdomyosarcoma has a network of fibrous septae that divide the cells into clusters or
aggregates, creating a crude resemblance to pulmonary alveoli.
Clinically, the tumor is usually aggressive, with unilateral proptosis when the orbit is involved.
Swelling and redness of the overlying skin, diplopia may be present. Immunohistochemistry is
positive for desmin, myoglobin, myogenin, MyoD1, and vimentin
2018
Treatment involves surgical resection and chemotherapy, with or without radiotherapy. The best
prognosis is for the botryoid variant while the pleomorphic has variant has the worst prognosis.
Other options:
A) Ewing's sarcoma: CD99 positive
C) Leukaemia: CD45 positive

subject: Pathology
question:
A 17-year-old boy presented with intussusception and was taken up for surgery. He was found
to have multiple polyps. The resected section was sent for histopathology and the whole
mount image of the specimen is given below. Identify the lesion.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-10-image]
explanation:
The given clinical feature of intussusception and multiple polyps and the histologic picture
showing an arborizing pattern of connective tissue, smooth muscle, lamina propria, and glands
are suggestive of Peutz–Jeghers Syndrome, which is a type of hamartomatous polyp of the small
intestine.
It is an autosomal dominant condition characterized by hamartomatous polyps of the intestinal
tract and melanosis of the mouth and the lips.
Clinical features:
• Hamartomatous polyps of the intestinal tract
• Hyperpigmentation of the buccal mucosa, lips, perianal skin, and digits
• Increased (2% to 10%) risk for cancer of the intestinal tract, with cancers reported throughout
the intestinal tract, from the stomach to the rectum.
• Increased risk for extraintestinal malignant neoplasms, including cancer of the breast, ovary,
cervix, fallopian tubes, thyroid, lung, gallbladder, bile ducts, pancreas, and testicles.
Management:
• Surgery is indicated in cases where there are bleeding, obstruction, intussusception, or
adenomatous changes of the polyp.
• If surgery is done for these indications attempts should be made to remove as much as polyps as
possible.
• Regular screening with endoscopic studies should be done due to the increased risk of
malignancies.
• Screening of other malignancies like breast and cervix are also important.
Other options:
A) Tubulovillous adenoma:
These are a mixture of tubular and villous adenoma and consist of small, pedunculated polyps
composed of rounded or tubular glands and larger, sessile polyps which are covered by slender
villi. These adenomas are characterized microscopically by the presence of epithelial dysplasia.
The hallmarks of epithelial dysplasia are nuclear hyperchromasia, elongation and stratification.
2019

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-10-exp-image]
C) Adenocarcinoma:
Presents with occult bleeding, changes in bowel habits, or cramping and left lower quadrant
discomfort. Morphologically, they present as polypoid, exophytic masses or annular lesions that
produce ‘napkin-ring’ construction and luminal narrowing. Microscopically, these tumours consist
of tall columnar cells with elongated, hyperchromatic nuclei and necrotic debris in the gland
lumen.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-10-exp-image]
D) Hyperplastic polyp:
Hyperplastic polyps, most commonly found in the left colon are smooth, nodular protrusions of
the mucosa, often on the crests of mucosal folds. Histologically, they are composed of mature
goblet and absorptive cells with a serrated surface architecture.
2020

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-10-exp-image]

subject: Pathology
question:
1999
Which of the following is true?
explanation:
Of the given statements, 2 and 3 are true. BRCA1 is a tumor suppressor gene and MYC is a
proto-oncogene.
BRCA1 is a tumor suppressor gene located on chromosome 17q21 and its function is to repair the
double-stranded breaks in DNA. It is associated with familial breast and ovarian carcinoma.
BRCA1-associated breast cancers are commonly poorly differentiated.
All breast cancers are separated into 3 major groups based on the expression of two proteins, ER
and HER2 (also known as ERBB2). HER2 cancers are defined as cancers overexpressing HER2
and can be either ER-positive or ER-negative. Thus HER2neu is expressed only in a fraction of
people with breast cancer.
EGF-receptor family has two proto-oncogenes: ERBB1 that codes for EGFR and ERBB2 that codes
for HER. Mutation in EGFR results in adenocarcinoma of the lung, which is one of the main
subtypes of non-small cell lung carcinoma.
MYC is a proto-oncogene, that is expressed in all eukaryotic cells and belongs to the
immediate-early response genes, which are rapidly induced by RAS/MAPK signaling following
growth factor stimulation of quiescent cells. Normally, MYC protein concentration are tightly
controlled at the level of transcription, translation and protein stability. The mechanisms by
which MYC promotes neoplastic cell growth may be follows:
• MYC activates the expression of many genes like D cyclins, that are involved in cell cycle
progression
• MYC regulates the expression of telomerase
• MYC is one of the transcriptional factors that can act together to reprogram somatic cells into
pluripotent stem cells
MYC gene is deregulated in a number of cancers such as Burkitt lymphoma, breast, colon, lung
and other cancers. The functionally identical NMYC and LMYC genes are also amplified in
2021
neuroblastomas and small cell cancers of the lung, respectively.

subject: Pathology
question:
Identify the given condition.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-12-image]
explanation:
The given histologic picture shows a suprabasal blister suggesting the diagnosis of pemphigus
vulgaris.
It is caused due to the production of antibodies mainly against desmoglein 3. Rarely antibodies
against desmoglein 1 can also be found. The antibodies are commonly IgG antibodies.
Desmoglein 3 is mainly present in the basal and suprabasal layers of the skin and mucosa. The
patients, therefore, present with flaccid bullae over the skin and mucosal ulcerations. Oral mucosa
is commonly affected. Other areas where ulceration can be seen are conjunctiva, nasopharynx,
larynx, etc. Cutaneous lesions are more common over the scalp, face, neck, upper chest, and back.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-12-exp-image]
Direct immunofluorescence will show the deposition of antibodies around the keratinocytes in the
epidermis. It is treated with steroids, azathioprine, mycophenolate mofetil, rituximab, etc.
2022

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-12-exp-image]
Other options:
B) Leprosy: Tuberculoid leprosy shows granulomatous lesions, without much bacilli. Hence called
paucibacillary leprosy. Lepromatous lesions contain large aggregates of lipid-laden macrophages
(lepra cells), often filled with masses (globi) of acid-fast bacilli. Because of the presence of
abundant bacteria, it is also called as multibacillary.
Given below are the images that depict lepromatous lesion showing foamy macrophages with
globi of pink/red acid-fast bacilli and tuberculoid leprosy showing granulomatous lesions,
respectively.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-12-exp-image]
2023

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-12-exp-image]
C) Mycosis fungoides: Mycosis fungoides is a cutaneous T-cell lymphoma, characterized by
polymorphic patches and plaques with fine scaling usually present on the limbs, breast, and
buttocks. The lesions may be pruritic or even asymptomatic.  Histological hallmarks include: 1.
Sézary-Lutzner cells - cells with cerebriform nuclei (infolded/ hyper-convoluted nuclear
membrane), 2. Pautrier's microabscesses - tumor cells invade the epidermis as single cells and
small clusters, forming a collection of atypical lymphocytes in the epidermis and 3.
Epidermotropism - pronounced invasion of the tumor cells into the epidermis.
Given below is the image of Sezary-Lutzner cells.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-12-exp-image]
Given below is the arrow pointing at Paurtier's microabcess.
2024

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-12-exp-image]
D) Visceral leishmaniasis: In visceral leishmaniasis, parasites invade and activate the
macrophages throughout the mononuclear phagocyte system. Hence, these phagocytic cells are
enlarged and filled with leishmania species; many plasma cells are also present.
The given slide shows macrophages infested with intracellular Leishmania donovani bodies
(arrows) characterized by a kinetoplast and characteristic double dot appearance (Leishman
stain).

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-12-exp-image]

subject: Pathology
question:
A karyotyping image is given below. What are the clinical features most likely to be expected
in such patients?
2000

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-13-image]
explanation:
The given karyotyping image shows ‘47, XXY’ abnormality, which is seen in Klinefelter’s
syndrome. Gynecomastia and long limbs are seen in Klinefelter's syndrome.
Other clinical features seen in Klinefelter’s syndrome are:
2025
• Disproportionately long arms and legs,
• Gynecomastia
• Eunuchoid body habitus
• Mitral valve prolapse
• Mental retardation; IQ: 60-80,
• Minor developmental and learning disabilities,
• Lack of secondary sexual characteristics (due to a decrease in androgen production)
• Higher risk of breast carcinoma
• Testicular dysgenesis with small penis
• Infertility/azoospermia
• Type 2 diabetes mellitus
• Metabolic syndrome
• Higher risk of extragonadal germ cell tumors
Klinefelter syndrome:
• Most common sex aneuploidy in males
• Classically karyotype is 47, XXY. Other variants can have 48 XXXY; rarely, 49 XXXXY; or
mosaics with some cells containing normal 46, XY and others 47, XXY
• Results from meiotic nondisjunction of sex chromosomes (mostly during 1st meiotic division)
Note:
A variant of Klinefelter’s syndrome—49, XXXXY—presents with short stature

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-13-exp-image]
2026

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-13-exp-image]
Other options:
B) Flat occiput:
It is usually seen in Down’s syndrome or trisomy 21.
Clinical features: (Mnemonic: I C A PROBLEM Somewhere)
• Intellectual disability/Incurved or short fifth finger with clinodactyly
• Congenital heart disease
• Acute megakaryoblastic leukemia/Alzheimer's disease/Atlantoaxial instability/Atresia of the
duodenum
• Protruding tongue
• Round face
• Occiput flat/Open wide fontanelle/Open mouth
• Brachycephalic/Brushfield's spots
• Low (depressed) nasal bridge
• Epicanthal fold/Ears low set and dysplastic
• Mongoloid slant (oblique palpebral fissure)
• Simian palmar crease and a wider gap between first and second toes
2027

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-13-exp-image]

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-13-exp-image]
C) Webbing of neck and lymphedema:
It is usually seen in Turner’s syndrome.
Important features associated with Turner's syndrome: (mnemonic: See A Baby CLOWN Here)
• Short stature, Short fourth metacarpal, Sensorineural hearing loss, Shield chest
• Amenorrhea (primary), infertility
• Barr body absent
• Cardiac defects, Cubitus valgus, Congenital lymphedema
• L: Learning disabilities
• Ovaries underdeveloped (streak ovaries), Gonadoblastoma (increased risk if Y chromosome
material is present)
• Webbed neck
2028
• Nipples widely spaced
• Horseshoe kidneys

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-13-exp-image]
D) Short stature and polydactyly:
Short stature with polydactyly is most commonly seen in Patau syndrome or trisomy 13.
It's clinical features include:
• Cleft lip often midline
• Flexed fingers with postaxial polydactyly
• Ocular hypotelorism
• Bulbous nose
• Low-set, malformed ears
• Microcephaly
• Cerebral malformation, especially holoprosencephaly
• Microphthalmia
• Cardiac malformations
• Scalp defects
• Hypoplastic or absent ribs
• Visceral and genital anomalies
2029

subject: Pathology
question:
A 20-year-old athlete suddenly collapsed while playing football and was taken to the hospital
where he was declared dead. His autopsy image is given below. Which of the following is the
most likely cause of death?

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-14-image]

options.a:
Valvular heart disease due to RHD
2001
options.b: Sudden cardiac death due to arrhythmia
options.c: Sudden cardiac death due to Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
options.d: Sudden cardiac death due to atherosclerosis

explanation:
The given image shows asymmetric septal hypertrophy and along with the scenario, it is
suggestive of sudden cardiac death due to arrhythmia. Patients with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
have an increased risk of sudden cardiac death from ventricular tachyarrhythmias. In fact,
arrhythmias are the most important/most common cause of death in hypertrophic
cardiomyopathy.
Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is one of the most common causes of sudden, otherwise
unexplained death in young athletes. HCM is characterized by reduced stroke volume due to
impaired diastolic filling, which is a consequence of the massively hypertrophic left ventricle.
There is a dynamic obstruction to the left ventricular outflow as the anterior mitral leaflet moves
towards the ventricular septum during systole. This compromised cardiac output in combination
with a secondary increase in pulmonary venous pressure is the reason for exertional dyspnea at
times of increased oxygen demand.
Major clinical problems in HCM are atrial fibrillation, stroke, intractable cardiac
failure, ventricular arrhythmias and also sudden death. There is a harsh systolic ejection murmur
caused by the ventricular outflow obstruction. Mutations causing hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
(HCM) are most commonly seen in the genes encoding β-myosin heavy chain
(MHC/MYH7) or myosin-binding protein C (MYBP-C).
HCM is mostly an autosomal dominant disorder. The most important histologic features of HCM
myocardium are as follows:
• Massive myocyte hypertrophy particularly of transverse myocyte diameters
• Myofiber disarray including that of bundles of myocytes, individual myocytes, and contractile
elements
• Interstitial and replacement fibrosis
2030
Most of the patients can be treated with β blockers to decrease heart rate and contractility.
Implantable cardioverter-defibrillators are used in patients with increased risk of ventricular
arrhythmias. Some patients can also be treated by surgical excision of the septal muscle or by
carefully controlled septal infarction through a catheter-based infusion of alcohol.
The below-given images show normal cardiac myocytes and myocytes in HCM respectively.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-14-exp-image]

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-14-exp-image]
2031

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-14-exp-image]

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following can be prevented by transfusing irradiated RBCs?
explanation:
Transfusion of irradiated RBCs prevent the incidence of graft versus host disease (GVHD)
associated with the transfusion.
GVHD occurs when immunologically competent cells or their precursors are transplanted into
immunologically compromised recipients and the transferred cells recognize the alloantigens in
the host and attack host tissues. It is seen most commonly during hematopoietic stem cell
transplantation but may occur following transplantation of solid organs too.
GVHD maybe of two types: acute and chronic.
Acute GVHD occurs within days to weeks after allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.
It mainly involves the immune system and the epithelia of the skin (in the form of generalized
rash), liver( in the form of jaundice), and intestines (in the form of bloody diarrhea). But the
affected tissues are not heavily infiltrated by lymphocytes.
Chronic GVHD may follow acute GVHD or may occur insidiously. These patients have extensive
skin damage, with destruction of skin appendages and fibrosis of the dermis, resembling systemic
sclerosis. Cholestatic jaundice and esophageal strictures are also common. There will be
involution of the thymus and depletion of lymphocytes in the lymph nodes. There will also be
recurrent bouts of life-threatening infections. Some may even develop autoimmunity, owing to the
grafted CD4+ helper T cells reacting with host B cells and stimulating them, which may result in
autoantibodies production.
Depletion of donor T cells by irradiation, before transfusion can eliminate the occurrence of
GVHD. But irradiation also has its side effects such as recurrence of tumor in leukemic patients,
increased incidence of graft failures and EBV-related B-cell lymphoma.

subject: Pathology
question:
Which of the following statements are correct regarding the effect of Glucose-6-Phosphate
Dehydrogenase deficiency?
explanation:
2032
Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency causes decrease in reduced glutathione
and decreased NADPH.
G6PD is an important enzyme involved in the hexose monophosphate shunt. Defective enzyme
function reduced the ability of red cells to protect themselves against oxidative injuries, thereby
increasing lipid peroxidation and lead to hemolysis.
• G6PD reduces NADP to NADPH while oxidizing glucose-6-phosphate.
• This NADPH then provides reducing equivalents needed for conversion of oxidized glutathione
to reduced glutathione.
• This reduced glutathione acts as a cofactor in reactions that neutralize compounds such as
H2O2, thereby protecting against oxidant injury.
• Exposure of G6PD deficient red cells to oxidative stress leads to crosslinking of reactive
sulfhydryl groups on globin chains, which then becomes denatured and form membrane-bound
precipitates called ‘Heinz bodies’, thereby producing membrane damage.
• When these inclusion bearing RBCs pass through the splenic cords, macrophages pluck out these
Heinz bodies.
• The resultant cells are called ‘Bite cells’.
Clinical features:
• Episodic hemolysis, both intravascular and extravascular
• Hemolytic anaemia
• Hemoglobinemia
• Hemoglobinuria
The episode of hemolysis is self-limiting, since only the older RBCs are at risk for lysis, leaving the
G6PD-replete younger RBCs in circulation. The recovery phase is heralded by reticulocytosis.

subject: Pathology
question:
Which of the following is not likely to be seen in a patient with Paroxysmal Nocturnal
Hemoglobinuria (PNH)?
explanation:
Leukemia is least likely to be seen in patients with Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria (PNH).
Clinical manifestations of PNH include:
• Hemolytic anemia
• Pancytopenia
• Intermittent hemoglobinuria- acute hemolytic episodes occur at night owing to a slight fall in pH
during sleep
• Haemosiderinuria
• Venous thrombosis (a leading cause of death in PNH)
• Patients diagnosed with PNH can later develop aplastic anemia and vice versa
PNH is an acquired disorder resulting from the mutation in the phosphatidylinositol glycan
complementation group A gene (PIGA), an enzyme that is essential for the synthesis of
2033
membrane-associated complement regulatory proteins. PNH blood cells are abnormally
susceptible to lysis or injury by complement since they are deficient in three GPI- linked proteins
that regulate complement activity:
• Decay-accelerating factor or CD55
• Membrane inhibitor of reactive lysis or CD59 (Inhibitor of C3 convertase) and
• C8-binding protein
Diagnosis:
• Ham’s test: The abnormal sensitivity of red blood cells, leukocytes, and platelets to complement
can be demonstrated in vitro using red cell lysis at acidic pH.
• Flow cytometry detects the red cells that are deficient in GPI-linked proteins such as CD59.
Treatment:
Eculizumab, a monoclonal antibody, that prevents the conversion of C5 to C5a has been proven
effective in PNH. The definitive treatment is hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.

subject: Pathology
question:
Given below is an electron microscopic image of the lung tissue. A patient infected with
SARS-COV2 presents with ARDS. The infection would affect and expand which of the labeled
2002
layers?

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-18-image]
explanation:
The image given in the question demonstrates the electron microscopic image of the blood-air
barrier in the lungs. Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) has been set in this patient with
COVID-19. Damage to the endothelial (Labelled as A) cells, with inflammation, is the key
initiating event and the basis of lung damage. Neutrophils play a significant role in tissue injury in
this condition.
Shock lung also known as ARDS/HMD/ALI (acute lung injury)  is characterized histologically
by diffuse alveolar damage (DAD). The characteristic histological picture is that of a hyaline
membrane lining the alveolar walls. Edema, scattered neutrophils and macrophages, and necrotic
epithelial cells are also present.
Histologic findings in COVID-19 patients :
• Diffuse alveolar damage
• Inflammation with mononuclear cells
The given HP slide shows diffuse alveolar damage which is the most common finding in the
histopathological analysis of COVID-19 infected lungs with ARDS.
2034

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-18-exp-image]
The image given in the question demonstrates the electron microscopic image of the blood-air
barrier in the lungs. The structures that form the same are labeled in the image given below. The
side of location of endothelium can be deduced by the location of the RBC.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-18-exp-image]

subject: Pathology
question: What is the gold standard investigation for the diagnosis of myocarditis?
explanation:
An endo-myocardial biopsy is the gold standard investigation for the diagnosis of myocarditis.
Myocarditis can be due to
• Infections: Coxsackie A and B, HIV, C.diphtheriae, Ricketssiae, Candida, Protozoa and
helminths.
• Immune-mediated reactions: Post-viral, post-streptococcal, drug hypersensitivity (e.g: methyl
dopa, sulfinamides), transplant rejection, etc.,
2035
• Unknown: Sarcoidosis, Giant cell myocarditis
Active myocarditis is characterized by an interstitial inflammatory infiltrate associated with
degenerating or apoptotic myocytes.

subject: Pathology
question:
A child presented with bilateral proptosis. From the peripheral smear, he was suspected to
have chloroma. What is the next investigation of choice?
explanation:
The next investigation of choice, in a child suspected to have a chloroma, is a bone marrow
biopsy.
Chloroma, also called myeloid sarcoma, granulocytic sarcoma, or myeloblastoma is an
extramedullary tumor that occurs in 2% to 14% of patients with acute myeloid leukemia (AML).
Frequent sites of involvement are the skin, lymph nodes, gastrointestinal tract, bone, soft tissue,
testis, and peritoneum.
Isolated orbital involvement of myeloid sarcoma is rare. Proptosis is the most common
presentation. Other symptoms include lacrimal swelling, conjunctival mass lesion, retinal
hemorrhages, ptosis, dystopia. Decreased visual acuity and ocular movement restriction have also
been reported.
Most cases of chloroma can be diagnosed on peripheral smear examination, which is easily
available and is relatively inexpensive. Sometimes it may not be associated with any peripheral
smear abnormality, and hence bone marrow aspirate and biopsy should be done to confirm
AML. The diagnosis can also be made if Auer rods are detected. Myeloid sarcomas have
immunophenotypic features that are identical to AML.
Although myeloid sarcomas are radiosensitive, they are managed with systemic chemotherapy in
most cases, even in the absence of bone marrow involvement. Surgery and/or radiation therapy
may be indicated for symptomatic lesions or tumors causing local organ dysfunction or
obstruction.
The image shows Auer rods contained within myeloblasts.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-20-exp-image]
2036

subject: Pathology
question:
A person working in an industry for a long time developed progressive breathlessness. The
histopathological image of his lung biopsy is given below. What industry was he most likely
working in?

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-21-image]
explanation:
From the clinical history of progressive breathlessness and the histopathological image showing
asbestos bodies, ferruginous bodies, and fibrosis, the patient is most likely working in the asbestos
industry.
Asbestosis occurs due to the inhalation of crystalline hydrated silicates (asbestos). It is
characterized by diffuse parenchymal interstitial fibrosis. It usually begins in the lower lobes of
the lungs and may present with dyspnoea and cough, usually after 20-30 years of exposure. Other
asbestos-related diseases include pleural plaques, recurrent pleural effusions, lung carcinoma, and
mesothelioma.
Asbestos bodies are a hallmark finding. They appear as golden brown, fusiform, or beaded rods.
These are formed when the asbestos fibers are phagocytosed by the macrophages and are coated
by iron-containing proteinaceous material derived from phagocyte ferritin. Similar iron-protein
complexes coat other inorganic particulates other than asbestos which are termed as ferruginous
bodies.
The image below shows asbestos bodies.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-21-exp-image]
Other options:
Option A: Silicosis is due to the inhalation of crystalline silicon dioxide (silica) as seen in
individuals employed in sandblasting or rock mining. Macrophages phagocytose inhaled silica
particles, thereby activating and releasing inflammatory mediators (IL-1, IL-8) that cause
fibroblast recruitment. Subsequently, collagen is deposited in the interstitium.
Microscopy reveals coalescent nodules of whorled collagen fibers surrounded by a peripheral zone
of silica-laden macrophages.
Shown below is an image of a silicotic nodule with collagen whorls as seen in this condition.
2037

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-21-exp-image]
Option C: Anthracosis/coal workers' pneumoconiosis is a pulmonary condition seen in coal
miners. The inhaled carbon pigment is engulfed by alveolar or interstitial macrophages, which
accumulate in the connective tissue. Initially it causes simple coal workers pneumoconiosis with
little or no pulmonary dysfunction. With continued exposure, lung tissue undergoes progressive
massive fibrosis which is called as complicated coal workers pneumoconiosis. The upper lobes of
the lungs are commonly involved.
Microscopic features consist of coal-laden macrophages, black pigment, and dense collagenous
interstitial fibrosis.
The image below shows anthracosis- black pigment engulfed within macrophages as well as in the
form of deposits.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-21-exp-image]
Option D: Cotton dust causes byssinosis and can induce occupational asthma.
2038

subject: Pathology
question:
A patient presented with complaints of dyspnea on exertion for 1 month. Echocardiography
showed the presence of mitral stenosis and left ventricular hypertrophy. Identify the
histopathological finding given below and the probable diagnosis.
2004

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-22-image]
explanation:
The lesion shown in the histopathological slide is Aschoff's body and the most probable diagnosis
is rheumatic heart disease (RHD).
Aschoff's bodies are the lesions found in the heart during acute rheumatic fever. They are
composed of foci of T lymphocytes, activated macrophages called Anitschkow cells, and occasional
plasma cells. Anitschkow cells have abundant cytoplasm and central round-to-ovoid nuclei with
chromatin that is centrally condensed into a slender, wavy ribbon. Hence, they are also called
caterpillar cells.
Rheumatic fever is the leading cause of mitral stenosis. During acute rheumatic fever, Aschoff
bodies may be found in any of the three layers of the heart that results in pericarditis, myocarditis,
or endocarditis.
Chronic inflammation leads to diffuse thickening and fibrosis of the valve leaflets, often with
calcific deposits. Fibrinoid necrosis occurs due to inflammation of the endocardium and the
left-sided valves. Over these areas of necrosis, small vegetations called verrucae develop along the
lines of closure. Irregular thickenings present in the subendocardium are called MacCallum
plaques.
In chronic RHD, the mitral valve leaflet is thickened, with commissural fusion and shortening.
There is thickening and fusion of the tendinous cords. This can lead to narrowing the mitral
orifice, leading to a fish mouth appearance (button hole).
Some major manifestations of rheumatic Fever are:
• Migratory polyarthritis of the large joints
• Pancarditis (myocarditis, pericarditis, or endocarditis)
• Subcutaneous nodules (typically on extensor surfaces of extremities)
• Erythema marginatum, an irregular circinate skin rash
• Sydenham chorea, a neurologic disorder with involuntary rapid movements
Image showing button hole appearance seen in mitral stenosis in case of rheumatic heart disease.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-22-exp-image]
2039
Image show Aschoff bodies seen in RHD.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-22-exp-image]
Other options:
Option B: Degenerative calcific deposits in the mitral valve typically develop in the fibrous
annulus. They are seen grossly as irregular, stony hard, occasionally ulcerated nodules at the base
of the leaflets. Usually, it does not affect valvular function. However, occasionally it can lead to
regurgitation, stenosis, or arrhythmias.
The image below shows severe calcification of the mitral valve annulus with focally thickened
shortened and fused chordae.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-22-exp-image]
Option C: In sarcoidosis, the involved tissues contain well-formed non-necrotizing granulomas.
They are composed of aggregates of tightly clustered epithelioid macrophages, often with giant
cells, usually without central necrosis. As the lesions become chronic, fibrous rims or hyaline
fibrous scars enclose the granulomas.
The image below shows non-necrotizing granulomas in the wall of a bronchiole in sarcoidosis.
2040

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-22-exp-image]
Option D: In tuberculosis, sites of active involvement of tuberculosis are marked histologically by
a characteristic granulomatous inflammatory reaction composed of both caseating and
non-caseating tubercles. Usually, granuloma shows central granular caseation surrounded by
multinucleate giant cells. Central caseation may be absent in non-caseating granulomas. Staining
should be done to detect acid-fast bacilli when granulomas are present.
The image below shows a caseating granulomatous lesion with areas of caseation (on the right
half) bordered by collections of epithelioid cells, Langhans giant cells, and lymphocytes.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-22-exp-image]
Image showing non-necrotizing granuloma seen in pulmonary tuberculosis.
2041

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-22-exp-image]

subject: Pathology
question:
A patient with lytic lesions on the skull is suspected of a diagnosis of Langerhan cell
histiocytosis. Which of the following is a characteristic finding on electron microscopy?
explanation:
The presence of Birbeck granules in the cytoplasm is a characteristic finding of Langerhans cell
histiocytosis (LCH) on electron microscopy.
Birbeck granules have a dilated terminal end producing a tennis racket–like appearance.
It contains the protein langerin (CD207). It is seen on electron microscopy.
The image below shows Birbeck granules on electron microscopy seen in LCH.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-23-exp-image]
Histopathologically, Langerhans cells have a single large nucleus with a characteristic folded or
grooved nucleus (coffee-bean appearance) with small inconspicuous nucleoli. Birbeck granules are
seen in the cytoplasm. The tumor cells specifically express HLA-DR, S-100, and CD1a and
langerin (CD207) markers.
2042
The image below shows histopathological image of Langerhan cells with folded nuclei.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-23-exp-image]
Langerhans cell histiocytosis occurs due to the proliferation of a special type of immature
dendritic cells, called Langerhans cells. Their normal function is to present antigens. The most
common mutation is BRAFV600E gain-of-function mutation.
It is characterized by single or multiple osteolytic bone lesions and can also infiltrate nearly every
organ, like the skin (of the scalp), lungs, liver, spleen, bone marrow, or the central nervous
system.
LCH can present as the following clinical entities:
• Multifocal multisystem Langerhans cell histiocytosis (Letterer-Siwe disease)
• Unifocal and multifocal unisystem Langerhans cell histiocytosis (eosinophilic granuloma)- The
combination of bone defects, diabetes insipidus, and exophthalmos is called the
Hand-Schüller-Christian triad.
• Pulmonary Langerhans cell histiocytosis
The below image shows lytic lesion of skull seen in LCH.
2043

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-23-exp-image]

subject: Pathology
question:
A 60-year-old patient presented with scrotal swelling and ataxia. Which among the following
could likely be a cause?
explanation:
The most likely cause in the following case is Non-Hodgkin lymphoma(NHL). Diffuse large B cell
lymphoma (DLBCL), a type of NHL is the most common lymphoma. NHL affects the elderly age
group and extranodal presentation is common. DLBCL can involve CNS, and CNS involvement is
particularly common if there is involvement of the testes, paranasal sinuses, or bone marrow as
well.
Primary testicular lymphoma is common in NHL when compared to Hodgkin lymphoma. Primary
testicular lymphoma(PTL) is an aggressive and uncommon form of non-Hodgkin lymphoma(NHL)
usually affecting the elderly age group(>60y). It typically presents as a painless, firm testicular
mass. Constitutional symptoms are uncommon, and if present, warrant a systemic involvement.
Metastasis involves the central nervous system (CNS), skin, contralateral testis, and pleura
resulting in local complications.
Even though ataxia is seen as a paraneoplastic symptom due to cerebellar degeneration in
Hodgkin lymphoma, testicular involvement is very rare and so far only 5 cases have been
reported. Hodgkin lymphoma arises in a single node or chain of nodes and spreads first to
anatomically contiguous lymphoid tissues. The extranodal presentation is rare. The extranodal
disease can occur at any site like lung, liver, bone, and bone marrow is most common if it occurs.
The central nervous system and the testis are exceptionally rare sites for Hodgkin's lymphoma.
Thus Hodgkin's lymphoma can be ruled out.
Other options:
A) Glioblastoma multiforme is a highly malignant tumor, common in older adults in the fifth and
sixth decades. The most common locations are the frontal and temporal lobes. The tumor may
spread and invade the other hemisphere, resulting in a characteristic
butterfly appearance. Extracranial metastases of malignant gliomas are extremely rare due to:
• Absence of a lymphatic system in the brain and spinal cord for systemic dissemination
• Dense dura around intracranial veins, preventing the penetration of the tumor
2044
• Lack of nurturing stroma in other organs prevents the proliferation of glioblastoma cells
Therefore the tumor metastasizing to the testis is very much unlikely and glioblastoma can be
ruled out.
Image showing MRI(magnetic resonance imaging) of glioblastoma

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-24-exp-image]
Option D) Testicular cancer is the most common cancer in men aged from 15 to 35 years. Germ
cell tumors(GCT) account for 90%–95% of cases.  Non-seminomatous form (NSGCT) metastasizes
to the lungs, liver, central nervous system, and bone in order of frequency. The frequency of brain
metastases is very low, not exceeding 1.2%.
Note: This is a controversial question. A more detailed clinical history and examination than what
is provided in the stem are required for us to arrive at a definitive diagnosis.

subject: Pathology
question:
A patient with cirrhosis and ascites presented to you. The gross appearance of his liver is as
shown below. Which among the following is true about it?

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-25-image]

options.a: Dark areas represent necrosis, white areas represent viable liver tissue
options.b: Dark areas represent viable liver tissue, white areas represent necrosis

explanation:
The gross image of the liver shows a nutmeg appearance, seen in heart failure. The dark areas
represent necrosis, white areas represent viable liver tissue.
Nutmeg liver refers to the mottled appearance of the liver which resembles the cut section of
nutmeg. This occurs as a result of hepatic venous congestion following right heart failure. The
dark centrilobular zones reflect sinusoidal congestion. Congestion is seen around the central veins
usually from right-sided heart failure. The pale periportal zones reflect normal or fatty liver tissue.
The liver derives its blood supply from the portal venous system and the hepatic artery. The portal
triad constitutes:
• Branches of the hepatic artery
• Portal vein
• Bile duct
2045
The blood from the portal vein and the hepatic artery flow through the intervening sinusoids and
drains into the central vein. The central veins from adjacent lobules form interlobular veins,
which then unite as hepatic veins and finally drain into the inferior vena cava.
The hepatic lobule is divided into three zones:
• Zone 1 is the periportal area
• Zone 2 is the intermediate zone
• Zone 3 is the area closest to the central vein
Right-sided cardiac failure leads to passive congestion of the liver. Left-sided cardiac failure leads
to hypoperfusion and hypoxia of hepatocytes around central veins. The combination of
hypoperfusion and retrograde congestion causes centrilobular hemorrhagic necrosis, leading to
this mottled appearance of the liver, known as nutmeg liver.
The microscopic image below of a nutmeg liver shows perisinusoidal fibrosis, hepatic venule
dilation, and dilation of the sinusoids in zone III.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-25-exp-image]

subject: Pathology
question:
A 6-year-old boy came with bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy. What is the probable
diagnosis, cell, and virus associated with the image given below?
2006

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-26-image]
explanation:
The probable diagnosis, cell, and virus from the given clinical scenario and the histopathological
image are Hodgkin's lymphoma, Reed-Sternberg cells (RS cells), and Epstein-Barr virus (EBV)
respectively.
Hodgkin's lymphoma arises in a single lymph node or chain of nodes and spreads first to
anatomically contiguous lymphoid tissues. It usually presents as painless lymphadenopathy. The
distinctive feature of Hodgkin's lymphoma is the presence of Reed-Sternberg cells.
RS cells are giant neoplastic cells derived from germinal centre or post–germinal centre B cells.
They appear as large cells with multiple nuclei or a single nucleus with multiple nuclear lobes.
Each cell contains large inclusion-like prominent nucleoli, and abundant, usually slightly
eosinophilic, cytoplasm, depicting an owl-eye appearance, as shown in the image below.
2046

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-26-exp-image]
WHO classifies Hodgkin's lymphoma into 5 subtypes:
• Nodular sclerosis: Frequent lacunar cells
• Mixed cellularity
• Lymphocyte-rich
• Lymphocyte depletion
• Nodular lymphocyte predominance
The first 4 subtypes are considered classical Hodgkin's lymphoma. The RS cells are CD 15+ and
CD30+. The lymphocyte predominant type is considered non-classical Hodgkin's lymphoma with
CD15-, CD30-, CD20+ and EB -.
The image below shows a popcorn cell seen in lymphocytic predominant Hodgkin's lymphoma.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-26-exp-image]
Other options:
Option A: RS cells are diagnostic of Hodgkin's lymphoma, not non-Hodgkin's lymphoma. The
histopathologic findings in non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL) are varied and depend on the subtype.
Option B: Kaposi sarcoma is a vascular neoplasm caused by human herpesvirus 8 (HHV8), also
known as Kaposi sarcoma herpesvirus. Cutaneous lesions of Kaposi sarcoma are composed of
spindle cells, mostly in the dermis or subcutaneous tissues, with small vessels and slit-like spaces
with red cells.
The image shows lesions seen in Kaposi's sarcoma.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-26-exp-image]
Option D: Infectious mononucleosis is caused by EBV. The peripheral blood smear shows absolute
lymphocytosis with mononucleosis cells which are atypical activated T lymphocytes. They have
abundant cytoplasm with multiple vacuolations and azurophilic granules, and an oval, indented,
or folded nucleus and are shown below.
2048

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-26-exp-image]
About 5-7% of the cases of infectious mononucleosis can be caused by the cytomegalovirus (CMV),
which can also cause pneumonia. CMV-infected pneumocytes with large, intranuclear, basophilic
inclusions with haloes at their periphery as well as small, granular cytoplasmic inclusions are seen
below with an owl-eye appearance.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2021-26-exp-image]

subject: Pathology
question:
A person was brought to the emergency department with facial swelling, itching, and
hypotension following a bee sting. The mentioned clinical features are due to the increase in
which immunoglobulin?
explanation:
The given clinical features suggest type 1 hypersensitivity reaction and are mediated by
immunoglobulin E (IgE).
Immediate (Type I) hypersensitivity reactions refer to allergic reactions or allergies.
