activate caspase 1 which cleaves the IL-1 precursor and releases its biologically active form.

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following features is not observed in pleomorphic adenoma?
explanation:
Perineural invasion is not observed in pleomorphic adenoma. Perineural invasion is seen in
malignant salivary gland tumours like adenoid cystic carcinoma.
Pleomorphic adenoma is the most common salivary gland and lacrimal gland neoplasm. They are
commonly seen in the parotid. They are painless, slow-growing, mobile, discrete masses within
the parotid.
Histologically, pleomorphic adenoma consists of epithelial and mesenchymal components.
• Epithelial components consist of
• Ductal cells, myoepithelial cells organized as ducts, acini, irregular tubules, or sheets of cells
• Squamous metaplasia with the formation of keratin pearls may be present
• Mesenchymal components consist of
• Loose myxoid and hyaline tissue containing cartilage (due to chondroid differentiation) and bone
The image below shows pleomorphic adenoma of the salivary gland consisting of mixed epithelial
(left) and mesenchymal cell components (right).

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-18-exp-image]
Clinical features
• Painless, slow-growing swelling
• Lobulated, firm, elastic-to-hard in consistency
• Lift the ear lobe
2129
• Free from deeper structures
FNAC is the investigation of choice in diagnosing pleomorphic adenoma. Open biopsy
contraindicated due to chances of injury to the facial nerve. Surgery is the mainstay of treatment 
by performing superficial parotidectomy.

subject: Pathology
question:
A 60-year-old woman presents with complaints of fatigue and dyspnea for 2 weeks. On
examination, pallor was seen and hemoglobin was found to be 6 gm/dL. What finding will be
seen on peripheral smear?
explanation:
Microcytic hypochromic cells will be seen on the peripheral smear. An elderly female presenting
with fatigue and dyspnea with severe anaemia is suggestive of iron deficiency anaemia(IDA). The
peripheral smear of IDA shows microcytic hypochromic red blood cells.
Iron deficiency is the most common nutritional disorder in the world. IDA results from dietary
deficiency, impaired absorption, increased requirement and, chronic blood loss. The cause of iron
deficiency anaemia in an older man or post-menopausal woman is always gastrointestinal cancer
until proven otherwise.
Clinical presentation may include fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, cheilosis (fissures at the corners of the
mouth) and koilonychia (spooning of the fingernails).
Peripheral smear in IDA shows:
• Microcytic hypochromic red blood cells with an enlarged zone of central one-third pallor.
• Poikilocytes: pencil-shaped RBCs and, target cells.
The image given below shows a microcytic hypochromic blood picture in a peripheral smear

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-19-exp-image]
IDA is treated by giving oral, parenteral and packed cell transfusions depending on the severity of
anaemia.
For oral iron replacement therapy,
• Up to 200mg of elemental iron is given.
2130
• According to the Anemia Mukt Bharat initiative, adult women need to take 60 mg elemental iron
+ 500 mcg folic acid tablets once a week.
For parenteral iron therapy, the amount of iron needed by an individual is calculated by the
formula:
Body weight(kg) x 2.3 x (15 - patient's hemoglobin, g/dL) + 500 or 1000mg (for stores)

subject: Pathology
question:
The image below is indicative of which of the following tumors?
2091

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-20-image]
explanation:
The image shows the characteristic Schiller Duval body consistent with a yolk sac tumor.
A yolk sac tumor/endodermal sinus tumor is an aggressive malignant germ cell tumor of the
ovaries. It originates from embryonal structures of the yolk sac by selective differentiation.
It is most oftenly seen in children and young women. It usually presents as abdominal
distention and a palpable pelvic mass.
Grossly, the yolk-sac tumor appears yellow, friable (hemorrhagic), and forms solid mass.
Most of the yolk sac tumors have Schiller-Duval bodies and they are pathognomonic. These are
glomerulus-like structures consisting of a central vessel surrounded by tumor cells within a space
that is also lined by tumor cells.
The image given below shows Schiller-Duval bodies seen in yolk sac tumors.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-20-exp-image]
Yolk-sac tumors are rich in alpha-fetoprotein. Therefore, serum levels of alpha-fetoprotein can
serve as a reliable tumor marker in post treatment surveillance.
Other options:
Option B: Dysgerminoma is the ovarian counterpart of seminoma. They are unilateral tumors
composed of vesicular cells and clear cytoplasm. The tumor cells grow in cords or sheets that are
separated by scanty fibrous stroma. They are usually infiltrated by lymphocytes.
2131
Option C: Krukenberg tumor is the carcinoma of the stomach that metastasize to the ovary. It is
characterized by mucin-producing signet ring cells. The appearance of a signet ring often
originates from the gastric mucosa.
The image below shows signet-ring cells in Kruckenberg tumor.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-20-exp-image]
Option D: Granulosa cell tumours arise from granulosa cells which form part of the developing
ovarian follicle. They are unilateral in nature and the mass is cystic and encapsulated. Call-Exner
bodies are seen in granulosa cell tumours. These are gland like small structures with acidophilic
material and immature follicles.
The image given below shows Call-Exner bodies seen in granulosa cell tumours.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-20-exp-image]

subject: Pathology
question: In a patient with bare lymphocytic disease, graft vs host reaction does not occur due to:

options.c: MHC class II deficiency on lymphocytes
options.d: MHC class I deficiency on lymphocytes

explanation:
2132
In a patient with bare lymphocytic disease, graft vs host reaction does not occur due to MHC class
II deficiency on the lymphocytes.
Bare lymphocyte disease occurs due to mutations in transcription factors required for class II
MHC gene expression. This results in the lack of class II MHC molecules, leading to the impaired
development of CD4+ T cells. As these CD 4+ T cells are required for both for cellular immunity
and for assisting B cells, class II MHC deficiency results in a combined immuno- deficiency.

subject: Pathology
question:
A child presents with multiple skin lesions over the inner thigh and genital areas. It initially
started as macule and later developed to become nodules. Some nodules have central
umbilication. The histology of the lesion is shown below. What would be found on biopsy?
2092

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-22-image]
explanation:
The given clinical scenario and histological image are diagnostic of molluscum contagiosum
lesions, which show intracytoplasmic inclusion bodies, called Henderson–Patterson bodies or
molluscum bodies.
Infection is usually spread by direct contact, particularly among children and young adults.
Multiple lesions occur on the skin and mucous membranes, with a predilection for the trunk and
anogenital areas. Individual lesions are firm, pruritic, pink to skin-colored umbilicated papules. A
curd-like material can be expressed from the central umbilication.
Smearing this material onto a glass slide and Giemsa staining shows diagnostic molluscum
bodies, which are ellipsoid, homogeneous, cytoplasmic inclusions that are found in the cells of the
stratum granulosum and the stratum corneum and are up to 35 µm in size.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-22-exp-image]
2133

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-22-exp-image]
Other options:
Option A: Multinucleate giant cells (called Tzanck cells) are seen in herpes simplex virus
infections. They are formed when individual epidermal cells containing viral inclusions fuse to
produce giant cells. These can be demonstrated by the diagnostic Tzanck test, which is based on
the microscopic examination of the vesicle fluid.
Option B: Donovan bodies are seen in donovanosis, caused by Klebsiella granulomatis. Donovan
bodies are minute, encapsulated coccobacilli in macrophages, which can be demonstrated in
Giemsa-stained smears of the exudate.
Option D: Koilocytes are characteristically seen in HPV infections, such as condylomata
acuminata. Koilocytes are lining cells that have perinuclear cytoplasmic vacuolization.

subject: Pathology
question:
Investigations of a patient being evaluated for leukemia revealed a WBC count of
4,50,000/µL, with eosinophilia and basophilia. What is the likely diagnosis in this case?
explanation:
A WBC count of 4,50,000/µL with eosinophilia and basophilia indicates a probable diagnosis of
chronic myelogenous leukemia.
CML is a myeloproliferative neoplasm. It is caused by the translocation of ABL gene on
chromosome 9 onto BCR gene on chromosome 22, resulting in the formation of the BCR-ABL
fusion gene. This chromosomal abnomality, called Philadelphia chromosome, is characteristic of
CML.
The resulting BCR-ABL translocation results in the activation of tyrosine kinase that signals
pro-growth and pro-survival pathways like RAS and JAK/STAT pathways resulting in the
proliferation of hematopoietic stem cells.
Patients typically present with a dragging sensation abdomen due to splenomegaly or splenic
infarct, fatigue, malaise, and weight loss.
On peripheral smear there is leukocytosis with leukocyte count, often exceeding 100,000
cells/mm3 (neutrophils, band forms, metamyelocytes, myelocytes, eosinophils, and basophils),
blasts are usually less than 10% of the circulating cells, the platelet count is usually increased.
2134
Basophils and eosinophils are often increased.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-23-exp-image]
Other options:
Option D: Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) is a hematopoietic progenitor malignancy that causes
immature myeloblast accumulation in the bone marrow.  Incidence increases with age and peaks
after 60 years of age.
The WBC count is about 15,000/µL. Few cases have either <5000 or >1,00,000/µL WBC
count. Clinically, the patients present with symptoms resulting due to pancytopenia (anemia,
neutropenia, thrombocytopenia). For acute myeloblastic leukemia (AML) diagnosis, the blast cell
count should be ≥20% in the peripheral blood or bone marrow
Other distinctive features of AML include
• Bone marrow/ peripheral smear shows a large, uniform population of cells (myeloblasts) with
prominent nucleoli (yellow arrow). It shows the presence of primary cytoplasmic granules
• Auer rods: needle-shaped azurophilic intracytoplasmic inclusion bodies formed by the fusion of
lysosomes. (black arrow)
• Multiple Auer rods clustered together within a single cell resembling a bundle of sticks known as
faggot cells (seen in peripheral smear).
2135

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-23-exp-image]
Option A: Acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) arises from immature B (pre-B) or T (pre-T) cells
called lymphoblasts. Most cases present with a reduced or normal lymphocyte count. Blood
counts show anemia, thrombocytopenia, and neutropenia.
Immunohistochemistry for terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase (TdT) is positive in pre-B and
pre-T lymphoblasts. Peripheral smear shows lymphoblasts that have condensed chromatin, less
conspicuous nucleoli, and smaller amounts of cytoplasm which lacks granules as shown below.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-23-exp-image]
Option C: Chronic lymphocytic leukemia presents with a mean WBC count of 30*109/L. It is
associated with the deletions of 13q, 11q, 17p, and trisomy 12q.
Lymph nodes are diffusely effaced by small lymphocytes with proliferation centers. The image
shows small round lymphocytes and smudge cells seen in CLL.
2136

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-23-exp-image]

subject: Pathology
question:
Which of the following molecules helps the leukocytes roll over the endothelial cells in the
vessels?
2093

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-24-image]
explanation:
E-selectin, which is present over the endothelium, helps the leukocyte roll over the endothelium.
Selectins are of 3 types: L-selectin present on leukocytes, E-selectin present on the endothelium,
and P-selectin present in platelets and on the endothelium.
The weak rolling action slows down the leukocytes and allows them to bind more firmly to the
endothelium with the help of integrins.
The recruitment of leukocytes to the site of injury involves the following steps:
• Lumen of the vessel to the endothelium:
• Margination: Leukocytes assume a peripheral position along the endothelial surface because of
the stasis of blood flow.
• Rolling: Leukocytes start rolling on the vessel wall because of signaling molecules from the
endothelium.
• Adhesion: Leukocytes are attached to the endothelium because of adhesion molecules that are
activated by cytokines.
• Transmigration of the leukocytes across the vascular endothelium.
• Migration towards a chemotactic stimulus in the extravascular tissues.
Other options:
Option A: Cadherins are a type of glycoprotein cell adhesion molecule that allows cells to adhere
together.
Option B: Platelet endothelial cell adhesion molecule-1 (PECAM-1), aids in diapedesis, which is the
movement of leukocytes through intact endothelium of the capillaries to reach the site of injury.
Option D: Cd11a, a molecule from the integrin family, helps firmly adhere the leukocyte to the
endothelial cell.
2137

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-24-exp-image]

subject: Pathology
question:
Identify the given bone marrow biopsy instrument.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-25-image]
explanation:
The given instrument is a Klima needle.
Klima and Salah's needles are reusable bone marrow aspiration & biopsy needles, both are
made of stainless steel. Klima needle has a guard that screws along the length of the needle. It has
no side screw. Whereas, Salah's needle has a guard situated 2 cm from the tip and has a side
screw.
The image below shows Klima needle.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-25-exp-image]
The image below shows Salah's needle.
2138

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-25-exp-image]
Islam and Jamshidi needles have a t-bar at the end for easier maneuvering. Jamshidi needle has a
tapering end to reduce the crush artefacts in the sample. Islam needle has a core securing device,
i.e., its cutting edge is shaped to hold the core of the bone during the procedure.
The image below shows the Jamshidi needle.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-25-exp-image]
The image below shows the Islam needle.
2139

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-25-exp-image]

subject: Pathology
question: Match the following stains and the tissue.
explanation:
The correct match is 1- a, 2- b, 3- d, 4- c.
Perl's Prussian blue stain is used in histopathological studies to detect the presence of iron in the
specimen. Iron is stained blue with this stain.
It is used in hereditary hemochromatosis to stain liver tissue, which shows hepatocellular iron. It
also stains the iron-laden mitochondria of ring sideroblasts seen in lead toxicity and
myelodysplastic syndrome.
The following image shows the blue staining of iron (in the form of hemosiderin) deposited in
hepatocytes:
1. Prussian blue stain
a. Iron
2. PAS stain
b. Glycog
en
3. Congo red stain
d. Amyloi
d
4. Fite- Faraco stain
c. Lepros
y
2140

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-26-exp-image]
PAS stain (Periodic acid Schiff stain) is used for the detection of glycogen in tissues such as the
liver, skeletal muscle, etc., and also in tumor cells.
The image below shows intense PAS +ve staining of hepatocytes in glycogen storage disorder:

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-26-exp-image]
The stain used for amyloid deposits is congo red staining, which imparts pink to red color to the
amyloid under ordinary light and apple-green birefringence under polarized microscopy.
The image shows a congo-red stained biopsy specimen with the following:
(A) Focal positive staining (orange-red) of amorphous extracellular material
(B) Apple-green birefringence under polarized light
2141

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-26-exp-image]
Fite-Faraco stain has been used in cases of leprosy for staining Mycobacterium leprae. It can also
be used to stain Nocardia.
The image below shows acid-fast bacilli inside foamy macrophages:

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-26-exp-image]

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following statements are true about hereditary spherocytosis?

options.a: Gall stones develop by 4-5 years of age.
options.b: Splenectomy does not protect against developing gall stones.
options.c: At least 25 % cases have a family history of HS.
options.d: High MCH is pathognomonic of HS.

explanation:
Among the given statements about hereditary spherocytosis, the true statement is option c– at
least 25% of cases have a familial inheritance (as 75% of cases show an autosomal dominant
inheritance pattern)
Hereditary spherocytosis is a type of hereditary hemolytic anemia that is mostly inherited in an
autosomal dominant pattern. Some of the cases can be autosomal recessive or
sporadic. Mutations of the red blood cell surface proteins spectrin and ankyrin can result in
2142
unstable cell membranes, leading to hemolysis.
The following mutations have been identified in hereditary spherocytosis:
Autosomal dominant (more common):
• Ankyrin - most common
• Band 3
• β-Spectrin
Autosomal recessive:
• α-Spectrin
• Band 4.2
This disorder can present at a young age with severe jaundice and fatigue. The unconjugated
hyperbilirubinemia predisposes the patient to develop gallstones in early adulthood. Pigment
stones are found in 40-50% of the affected adults.
Laboratory findings include normocytic anemia with unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia. The
peripheral smear will show large spherical red blood cells without central pallor.
MCHC (not, MCH) is increased in hereditary spherocytosis; however, it is not pathognomonic as it
could be found in other conditions as well. MCV is mildly decreased because of cell
shrinkage. There is mild to moderate anemia, therefore, MCH could be slightly lower.
The diagnosis can be confirmed by the acid glycerol lysis test and the eosin-5′-maleimide
(EMA)–binding test.
There is no specific treatment available besides splenectomy. Spleen is the major site where
spherocytes undergo hemolysis. Hence, splenectomy corrects the anemia and its associated
complications, thereby playing a role in preventing gallstones. It is done at 4–6 years of age in
severe cases and after puberty in moderate cases. It should be followed up with the appropriate
antibiotic and vaccine prophylaxis.

subject: Pathology
question: B cells express IgM and IgD antibodies at the same time due to which phenomenon?
explanation:
B cells express IgM and IgD antibodies at the same time due to alternative RNA splicing.
Alternative RNA splicing is the process by which different proteins are produced from the same
RNA transcript by splicing it in different ways.
Mature circulating B cells initially express IgM and IgD antibodies on their surfaces before they
are stimulated to produce specific antibodies. The heavy chains in IgM and IgD are mu (µ)
and delta (δ), respectively. The mu and delta genes for these heavy chains are located proximal to
the VDJ encoding regions (i.e., variable, diversity, and joining regions) of the heavy chain locus. A
primary RNA transcript of this entire region is produced, which then undergoes alternative RNA
processing, enabling the production of both transmembrane IgM and IgD on B cells.
Other options:
Options A and C: Somatic hypermutation is a cellular response by which the immune system
adapts to the new foreign elements that confront it. They do so by adding nucleotides in V gene
2143
regions of heavy and light chains, resulting in a better fit of antigen with the antibodies. This
hence increases the affinity of antibodies to antigens. Due to this, the subset of plasma cells with
these improved hypervariable regions are more strongly selected by the antigen and therefore
constitute a larger part of the population of antibody-producing cells. This process is called
affinity maturation.
Option D: Allelic exclusion is the process by which a cell expresses one of the two alleles of the
gene and excludes the other allele.
B cells are diploid, therefore, each B cell has six gene segment pools related to antibody chains:
• Two heavy-chain pools (one from each parent) and
• Four light-chain pools (one κ and one λ from each parent).
However, each B cell uses only two of the six gene segment pools: one out of the two heavy-chain
pools (maternal and paternal) and one out of the four light-chain pools. Thus, each B cell must
choose not only between its κ and λ light-chain pools but also between its maternal and paternal
light-chain and heavy-chain pools. This process of choosing is by allelic exclusion.

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following are true regarding sirtuins?
explanation:
The true statements regarding sirtuins are 1, 2, 3, and 4 (option B).
Sirtuins are a class of proteins that possess either mono-ADP-ribosyltransferase or deacetylase
activity. They have been shown to slow down the aging process.
Sirtuins promote the expression of several genes whose products increase longevity. These include
proteins that:
• Inhibit metabolic activity
• Reduce apoptosis
• Stimulate protein folding
• Inhibit the harmful effects of oxygen-free radicals
• Increase insulin sensitivity and glucose metabolism (maybe targeted for the treatment of
diabetes)
• Ensure that damaged DNA is not propagated, preventing the accumulation of mutations
• Promotes replicative senescence under chronic stress
Sirtuins level increases by caloric restriction and are present in red wine.
Sirtuins are responsible for an increase in insulin sensitivity. Hence, they can play an important
role in the treatment of diabetes.

subject: Pathology
question: Match the following cells with their locations.
explanation:
The correctly matched set of the cells and their locations is 1-b, 2-a, 3-c, 4-d.
2144
Kupffer cells are a component of the mononuclear phagocytic system and are located in the liver.
Parietal cells, also called oxyntic cells, are found in the upper half of the gastric glands. They have
numerous mitochondria and are actively involved in the secretion of hydrochloric acid and
intrinsic factor - a glycoprotein that aids in the absorption of vitamin B12 from the ileum.
Dust cells are macrophages in the walls of the alveoli. They phagocytose dust particles in the
alveoli. In CHF these macrophages phagocytose erythrocytes that escape from the capillaries. The
cells acquire a brick-red color and are called heart failure cells.
Clara cells are non-ciliated cuboidal epithelial cells that secrete important defense markers and
serve as stem cells after injury. These cells make up a larger portion of the epithelial lining in the
later portions of the conducting airway.

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following cytokines is not involved in Th1 response?
explanation:
The cytokine not involved in TH1 response is IL-4.
Dendritic cells and macrophages present antigens to T helper cells (TH) after which they
differentiate into TH1 and TH2 cells. They are responsible for cell-mediated and humoral immune
responses, respectively. IL-12 is secreted by macrophages and promotes the proliferation of TH1
cells.
TH1 cells secrete:
• IL-2 which activates natural killer cells (NK cells) and cytotoxic T cells
• IFN gamma which stimulates macrophages and is also responsible for delayed hypersensitivity
along with TGF beta.
TH2 cells secrete:
• IL- 4 stimulates IgE production from B cells
• IL- 5 stimulates proliferation of eosinophils and together play a role in helminthic and allergic
reactions
• IL- 6 promotes proliferation of B cells and production of antibodies.

subject: Pathology
question:
A 19-year-old girl presents with a midline neck swelling that moves on deglutition. USG
revealed a 2 cm x 2 cm nodule on the left lobe of the thyroid, extending into the strap
muscles. The histopathology image is given below. What is the most likely diagnosis?

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-32-image]
2096
explanation:
The clinical scenario and the image showing papillae lined by cells with overlapping nuclei
and Orphan Annie eye nuclei indicate a diagnosis of papillary carcinoma of thyroid.
Papillary carcinoma of thyroid presents as a painless nodule or mass in the neck or cervical node
in women of reproductive age. The risk factors include exposure to ionizing
radiation and Hashimoto thyroiditis.
It usually presents as a cold nodule on a scan. Histopathological examination shows branching
papillae and follicles with fibrovascular stalk. Nuclear changes include ground glass or Orphan
Annie eye nuclei, pseudo-inclusions, and nuclear grooves. Psammoma bodies are often present.
2145
Diagnosis can be made based on these nuclear features, even in the absence of papillary
architecture. The prognosis is excellent, with a 10-year survival rate in excess of 95%.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-32-exp-image]
The image below shows Orphan Annie eye nuclei with central clearing and margination of
chromatin:

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-32-exp-image]
Other options:
Option A: Medullary carcinoma of the thyroid is a neuroendocrine tumor that is derived from
parafollicular C cells, that secrete calcitonin.
Most of these tumors occur sporadically. Some are associated with familial multiple endocrine
neoplasia (MEN) syndrome: MEN 2A and MEN 2B. They frequently present as a neck mass that
may be associated with cervical lymphadenopathy. With the enlargement of the nodule, patients
may have dysphagia and dyspnea.
Histopathology shows polygonal to spindle-shaped cells which form nests, trabeculae, or follicles.
Amyloid deposits that are derived from the altered calcitonin molecules are present in the stroma.
2146
Calcitonin in the tumor cells and stroma is easily demonstratable by immunohistochemistry.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-32-exp-image]
Option B: Follicular carcinoma thyroid on histopathology may show uniform cells forming small
follicles containing colloid (resembling normal thyroid gland) or sheets of cells without colloid.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-32-exp-image]
Anaplastic carcinomas are poorly differentiated thyroid carcinomas with varied morphology
including giant cells or spindle-shaped cells or both. There is minimal follicular differentiation
with intratumoral necrosis.
2147

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-32-exp-image]
Option C: A thyroglossal duct cyst is a congenital midline neck swelling that typically presents
during childhood often noticed after an upper respiratory infection.
It represents the persistence of the track of the thyroid gland and can be found adjacent to
the midline. It presents as smooth, fluctuant (cystic) swelling that is often transilluminant.
Classically, the cyst moves upwards on swallowing and on tongue protrusion.
Sistrunk’s operation is performed to excise the cyst and prevent a recurrence.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-32-exp-image]

subject: Pathology
question:
A chronic smoker with a smoking history of 24 packs presents with breathlessness and chest
pain. Further investigations revealed the presence of a lung tumor. On staining, cells are
positive for chromogranin. What is the diagnosis?
explanation:
The clinical scenario of a lung tumor associated with chronic smoking and tumor cells staining
positive for chromogranin indicates that the most likely diagnosis in this patient is small cell
carcinoma of the lung.
2148
Small cell carcinoma of the lung belongs to the neuroendocrine tumors and originates
from neuroendocrine progenitor cells, Kulchitsky cells that line the bronchial epithelium. It is a
highly malignant tumor with a high association with cigarette smoking. It usually arises from the
major bronchi.
Histopathology of small cell lung carcinoma shows clusters of small cells which are round, oval, or
spindle-shaped, and a prominent nuclear molding. These cells have scant cytoplasm, ill-defined
cell borders, finely granular nuclear chromatin (salt and pepper pattern), and absent or
inconspicuous nucleoli. The Azzopardi effect is described as basophilic staining of vascular walls
due to coating by DNA from necrotic tumor cells.
Neuroendocrine markers seen in small cell lung carcinoma include:
• Chromogranin
• Synaptophysin
• CD56
Paraneoplastic syndromes associated with small cell carcinoma of the lung are Cushing syndrome
due to ACTH production and hyponatremia due to inappropriate ADH secretion. Small cell lung
cancer-associated SIADH is due to the ectopic release of antidiuretic hormone. It is associated
with increased thirst and hyponatremia.
Other options:
Option A- Adenocarcinoma is a malignant epithelial tumor with differentiation in glands or mucin
production by the tumor cells. These tumors are commonly found in women and in non-smokers.
These tumors are more peripherally located and are smaller. On histopathology, the tumor shows
a lepidic pattern of spread with tumor cells crawling along the normal-appearing alveolar septa.
The tumor cells are positive for thyroid transcription factor 1 (TTF-1) and Napsin A.
Option B- Squamous cell carcinoma is found to be more common in men and has a strong
association with smoking. They have precursor lesions like squamous dysplasia, squamous
metaplasia, or in situ carcinomas. On histopathology, it is characterized by keratinization and/or
intercellular bridges. Keratinization occurs in the form of squamous pearls. It is positive for p40
and p63 markers. Hypercalcemia is the common paraneoplastic syndrome associated with this
type.
Option C- Large cell carcinoma is an undifferentiated malignant epithelial carcinoma. On
histopathology, the cells are large, with large nuclei, prominent nucleoli with a moderate amount
of cytoplasm.

subject: Pathology
question:
A male patient presented with hemoptysis. On examination, hilar mass was present. The
histopathological image is shown below. Immunohistochemistry revealed that the cells were
positive for p40. What is the diagnosis?

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-34-image]
explanation:
The given clinical history and the histopathological findings showing keratin pearls are suggestive
of squamous cell carcinoma of the lung.
Squamous cell carcinoma is most commonly found in men and is strongly associated with
smoking.
Squamous cell carcinoma predominantly arises in the central segmental or subsegmental
bronchi. The tumor may grow into the bronchial lumen and produce an intraluminal mass. In
2149
certain cases, the bronchial obstruction may lead to atelectasis or infection. The tumor may
penetrate the wall of the bronchus and infiltrate the peribronchial tissue into the carina or the
mediastinum.
This tumor may be associated with paraneoplastic syndromes like hypercalcemia. The symptoms
could include anorexia, nausea, vomiting, constipation, lethargy, polyuria, polydipsia, and
dehydration.
Squamous metaplasia and dysplasia often precede frank squamous cell carcinoma. Carcinoma
in-situ persists for many years and this may often be recognized on cytologic smears or in BAL
fluid or brushings.
Gross appearance: The tissue is grey-white, firm, with areas of necrosis and hemorrhages. Red or
yellow-white mottling and softening may be seen. The necrotic foci may cavitate.
Histopathology: They have precursor lesions like squamous dysplasia, squamous metaplasia, or in
situ carcinomas. On histopathology, it is characterized by keratinization and/or intercellular
bridges. Keratinization occurs in the form of squamous pearls or individual cells with dense
eosinophilic cytoplasm. It is positive for p40 and p63 markers.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-34-exp-image]

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following is true about proto-oncogenes?
explanation:
The true statement about proto-oncogenes is statement 3. ERBB2 amplification (gene that
encodes HER2) is associated with breast cancer, while ERBB 1 mutation (gene that encodes
EGFR) is associated with adenocarcinoma of the lung.
Other statements:
Statement 1: The most common genetic change in GISTs is the gain-of-function mutation of the
tyrosine kinase KIT gene. These are present in 75% to 85% of all GISTs. Therefore KIT has
increased expression in GIST.
The second most common mutation results in the activation of platelet-derived growth factor
receptor A (PDGFRA).
2150
Statement 2: KRAS mutation is associated with colon, lung, and pancreatic tumors. Among lung
cancers, about 30% of cases of adenocarcinoma have gain of function mutations of the KRAS
gene.
Small cell carcinomas are associated with the inactivation of TP53 and RB genes.
Statement 4: PDGFR-β amplification is associated with gliomas and leukemias. PDGF-β
overexpression is associated with astrocytoma.
Mutations involved in colon cancer pathogenesis:
• Familial adenomatous polyposis usually has mutations involving the APC gene.
• HNPCC- majority of the cases have mutations in MSH2 or MLH1.
• Adenocarcinomas- the two pathways implicated are the APC pathway and MSI pathway.

subject: Pathology
question:
The given peripheral smear is associated with which of the following leukemias?

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-36-image]
explanation:
The peripheral smear findings point toward a diagnosis of chronic lymphocytic leukemia.
The peripheral smear shows smudge cells, increased lymphocytes with scant cytoplasm, and
round to slightly irregular nuclei containing clumped chromatin. This large number of
monotonous, uniform-looking, mature lymphocytes is often described as a "convent-girl"
appearance.
Chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL)/ Small lymphocytic lymphoma (SLL) is the most common
leukemia among adults in the western world. The differentiating point between CLL and SLL is
that the absolute lymphocyte count is >5000/mm³ in CLL and <5000/mm³ in SLL.
The chromosomal defects:
• Deletion of 13q (commonest and has a good prognosis)
• Deletion of 11q
• Trisomy 12q
• Deletion of 17p (poor prognosis)
It is usually asymptomatic at diagnosis but can present with easy fatigability, weight loss, and
anorexia. Generalized lymphadenopathy and hepatosplenomegaly are usually seen.
Total blood count:
• Total leukocyte count is highly variable; leukopenia is seen when the marrow is involved.
• Hypogammaglobulinemia is common and contributes to increased susceptibility to infections.
• 10% to 15% of patients develop hemolytic anemia or thrombocytopenia due
to autoantibodies made by non-neoplastic B cells.
Findings of peripheral smear:
• Predominantly shows small lymphocytes with scant cytoplasm.
• Contains a large number of monotonous, uniform-looking, mature lymphocytes, which is often
described as a convent-girl appearance.
2151
• Smudge cells or basket cells or parachute cells - artifacts of the lymphocytes which are disrupted
during the process of making smears.
• Spherocytes (due to coexisting autoimmune hemolytic anemia) and nucleated RBCs (due to
premature release of progenitors in face of severe anemia, marrow infiltration by the tumor, or
both)

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-36-exp-image]
Lymph node biopsy: Diffuse effacement of lymph node architecture is seen. Small lymphocytes
admixed with variable numbers of larger activated lymphocytes that often gather in loose
aggregates. Proliferation centers containing mitotically active cells are pathognomonic for CLL /
SLL.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-36-exp-image]
The histopathological image shows small, darker staining lymphocytes with prominent nucleoli,
which are typically seen in chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL).
2152

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-36-exp-image]
AML is a hematopoietic progenitor malignancy that causes immature myeloblast accumulation in
the bone marrow. Incidence increases with age and peaks after 60 years of age.
Clinically, the patients present with symptoms resulting due to pancytopenia (anemia,
neutropenia, thrombocytopenia). There is generalized fatigue, bone pain, bleeding tendencies, and
an increased risk of recurrent infections. For acute myeloblastic leukemia (AML) diagnosis, the
blast cell count should be ≥20% in the peripheral blood or bone marrow.
Other distinctive features of AML:
• Bone marrow/ smear shows a large, uniform population of cells (myeloblasts) with prominent
nucleoli (yellow arrow). It shows the presence of primary cytoplasmic granules.
• Auer rods: needle-shaped azurophilic intracytoplasmic inclusion bodies formed by the fusion of
lysosomes. (black arrow)
• Multiple Auer rods clustered together within a single cell resembling a bundle of sticks known as
faggot cells (seen in peripheral smear).

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-36-exp-image]
2153
Acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) arises from immature B (pre-B) or T (pre-T) cells called
lymphoblasts. Immunohistochemistry for terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase (TdT) is positive
in pre-B and pre-T lymphoblasts.
Peripheral smear shows lymphoblasts that have condensed chromatin, less conspicuous nucleoli,
and smaller amounts of cytoplasm which lacks granules as shown below.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-36-exp-image]
Chronic myeloid leukemia is characterized by the presence of a Philadelphia chromosome
resulting from reciprocal translocation t(9;22) forming the BCR-ABL fusion gene. They have
hypercellular marrow due to a massively increased number of maturing granulocytic precursors.
The image below shows peripheral smear findings of CML with neutrophils, myelocytes,
metamyelocytes, and band cells.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-36-exp-image]

subject: Pathology
question:
Which of the following statements are true about familial adenomatous polyposis?
2098
explanation:
2154
The true statements about familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) are statements 2 and 4 (option-
A).
Familial adenomatous polyposis is an inherited autosomal dominant disorder presenting
numerous colorectal adenomatous polyps. It is an autosomal dominant disease caused by
mutations in the APC gene, which is a regulator in the Wnt signaling pathway. The diagnosis of
FAP requires the presence of at least 100 polyps.
Patients are usually teenagers and multiple polyps are visible in the colon during sigmoidoscopy.
It is associated with a variety of extraintestinal manifestations including congenital hypertrophy of
the retinal pigmental epithelium. Colorectal adenocarcinoma occurs in almost all cases before the
age of 50.
Prophylactic colectomy is recommended in patients with APC gene mutations. The surgeries used
are colectomy with ileorectal anastomosis (IRA), restorative proctocolectomy with an ileal
pouch-anal anastomosis (RPC), or total proctectomy and end ileostomy. The first 2 surgeries are
preferred in young patients as it avoids a permanent stoma.
Note - FAP along with brain tumors is called Turcot syndrome. FAP along with multiple osteomas,
skin, and soft tissue tumors is called Gardner syndrome.

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following shows maximum amplitude on thromboelastography?
explanation:
The maximum amplitude in thromboelastography represents platelets. A decrease in maximum
amplitude signifies a quantitative or qualitative deficiency of platelets.
Thromboelastography (TEG) is a non-invasive test that quantifies the ability of whole blood to
form a clot. It is useful in intraoperative coagulation status assessment. The dynamic changes in
the viscoelastic properties of a blood sample during the clotting can be detected and quantified by
thromboelastography. Underlying coagulation deficiencies show different shapes in the TEG. They
are shown in the image below:

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-38-exp-image]
2155

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-38-exp-image]
Advantages of thromboelastography:
•
Blood coagulability, including coagulation cascade, platelet function, and fibrinolysis can be
assessed.
•
It is a simple bedside test
•
Can diagnose coagulopathic bleeding
•
Can be used as a guide in transfusion therapy and decrease the use of blood products
•
Can detect dynamic changes in blood coagulation during resuscitation
•
Predict the clinical efficacy of therapeutic agents affecting blood coagulability

subject: Pathology
question:
A man presented with fever, night sweats, and weight loss for 2 months. On examination,
non-tender, rubbery lymph nodes were palpable in the supraclavicular and inguinal regions
on both sides. Peripheral smear showed the presence of bi-nucleated cells. Which markers
will be seen in this condition?
explanation:
The probable diagnosis from the given clinical scenario and the histopathological description
is Hodgkin's lymphoma. The bi-nucleated cells are called the Reed-Sternberg cells which are CD
15+ and CD30+ (option D- 1 and 2).
Hodgkin's lymphoma arises in a single lymph node or chain of nodes and spreads first to
anatomically contiguous lymphoid tissues. It usually presents as painless lymphadenopathy. The
distinctive feature of Hodgkin's lymphoma is the presence of Reed-Sternberg cells.
WHO classifies Hodgkin's lymphoma into 5 subtypes:
2156
• Nodular sclerosis: Frequent lacunar cells
• Mixed cellularity
• Lymphocyte-rich
• Lymphocyte depletion
• Nodular lymphocyte predominance
The first 4 subtypes are considered classical Hodgkin's lymphoma. The RS cells are CD 15+ and
CD30+. The lymphocyte predominant type is considered non-classical Hodgkin's lymphoma with
CD15-, CD30-, CD20+, and EB -.
The image below shows various cells like eosinophils, RS cells, plasma cells, and histiocytes in an
FNA specimen of Hodgkin lymphoma:

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-39-exp-image]

Reed-Sternberg cell (RS cell) is the characteristic tumor giant cell of Hodgkin Lymphoma. It is
derived from the germinal or post-germinal center B cell. It has a classic owl-eyed appearance due
to the presence of a symmetric or mirror-image bilobed nucleus, with prominent nucleoli
surrounded by a clear space. It has the following variants:
• Lacunar
• Classic
• L&H popcorn cells
• Pleomorphic
• Mononuclear
RS cells should be present in a background of non-neoplastic inflammatory cells like lymphocytes,
plasma cells, and eosinophils, to make a diagnosis of Hodgkin’s lymphoma.
The image given below shows the diagnostic Reed-Sternberg cell, with two nuclear lobes, large
inclusion-like nucleoli, and abundant cytoplasm, surrounded by lymphocytes, macrophages, and
an eosinophil.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-39-exp-image]
The image below shows the Reed-Sternberg cell, mononuclear variant.
2158

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-39-exp-image]
The image below shows Reed-Sternberg cell, lacunar variant. This variant has a folded or
multilobated nucleus and lies within an open space, which is an artifact created by disruption of
the cytoplasm during tissue sectioning.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-39-exp-image]
The image below shows the Reed-Sternberg cell, lymphohistiocytic variant. Several such variants
with multiple infolded nuclear membranes, small nucleoli, fine chromatin, and abundant pale
cytoplasm are present.
2159

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-39-exp-image]

subject: Pathology
question:
A 10-year-old boy presented with fatigue. Investigations revealed: Hemoglobin, 9 g/dL; MCV,
60 fL; MCH, 20 pg; and serum ferritin, 185 µg/L. The TLC was elevated and showed
predominant lymphocytes and neutrophils. What is the likely diagnosis in this patient?
explanation:
The given clinical scenario and the investigation findings are suggestive of microcytic
hypochromic anemia. The most likely diagnosis in this patient is anemia of chronic disorder.
Anemia of chronic disorders more commonly presents as normochromic normocytic anemia that
later develops into microcytic hypochromic anemia rarely. It is caused due to production of
erythrocytes.
Anemia of chronic disease is anemia resulting due to chronic diseases which produce systemic
inflammation. During chronic inflammation, inflammatory mediators like interleukin - 6
(IL-6) stimulate the production of hepcidin from the liver. Hepcidin is an acute phase
reactant that inhibits ferroportin on macrophages and also decreases transport of iron from stores
to developing RBC precursors. This results in decreased proliferation of erythroid progenitors due
to impaired iron utilization.
Other options:
Option B: Thalassemia also presents as iron deficiency anemia but it does not have elevated
luekocytosis.
Option C: Iron deficiency anemia also presents as microcytic hypochromic anemia. But since there
is deficient iron, the serum ferritin level will also be low. This patient has normal serum ferritin
and hence does not have iron deficiency anemia.
Note:
MCV/RBC count (Mentzer index) can also be used to distinguish thalassemia from iron-deficiency
anemia (IDA). MCV (mean corpuscular volume) and MCH (mean corpuscular hemoglobin) are
low in both IDA and thalassemia. RBC count may be normal in thalassemia, whereas it is reduced
in IDA. A lower RBC count in IDA means the denominator is smaller, yielding a greater MCV/RBC
ratio.
2160
Option D: Sickle cell anemia is caused by a mutation in β-globin that creates sickle hemoglobin
(HbS). It does not present as microcytic hypochromic anemia.

subject: Pathology
question: Senile atrophy occurs due to ______.
explanation:
Senile atrophy is seen when there is a reduced blood supply to a tissue. This reduction in the
blood supply occurs in a gradual manner because of slowly progressing atherosclerotic lesions.
Atrophy is a process of reduction in the size of a tissue or an organ resulting from a decrease in
the cell size and number. It is a type of cellular adaptation. In this process, there is a decrease in
the synthesis of cellular proteins and an increase in their degradation, mainly mediated by the
ubiquitin-proteasome pathway. Atrophy can either be physiological or pathological:
Physiologic atrophy:
• Loss of hormonal stimulation: Atrophy of the uterus after parturition; atrophy of the
endometrium, vaginal epithelium, and breast after menopause.
• During embryonic development: Certain structures, like the notochord and thyroglossal duct,
undergo atrophy during fetal development.
Pathologic atrophy:
• Disuse Atrophy (decreased workload): e.g., atrophy in a limb that has been immobilized
following a fracture
• Denervation atrophy (loss of nerve supply): e.g., skeletal muscle atrophy following nerve injury
• Ischemic atrophy: e.g., senile atrophy of the brain and heart due to atherosclerosis
• Inadequate nutrition: e.g., severe muscle wasting in marasmus
• Pressure atrophy: e.g., atrophy of surrounding uninvolved tissues caused by an enlarging benign
tumor

subject: Pathology
question: Cyclooxygenase plays a role in which pathway?
explanation:
Prostaglandins are produced from arachidonic acid present in the membrane phospholipids. This
process is mediated by 2 cyclooxygenases: COX-1 and COX-2. Prostaglandins are produced by
mast cells, macrophages, and endothelial cells.
COX-1 is commonly found in most tissues. It plays a role in maintaining normal homeostasis. It is
also induced by inflammatory stimuli.
COX-2 is found in cells that are involved in inflammatory reactions.
The most important prostaglandins in inflammation are PGE2, PGD2, PGF2a, PGI2
(prostacyclin), and thromboxane A2.
Other options:
Options A: Kreb's cycle is also called the citric acid cycle and tricarboxylic acid cycle. It is the
terminal pathway for the oxidative catabolism of carbohydrates, amino acids, and fatty acids.
2161
Option C: Leukotrienes are also produced from arachidonic acid with the help of lipoxygenases.

subject: Pathology
question: Metastatic calcification can be explained by all, except ______.
explanation:
Metastatic calcification cannot be explained by vitamin D deficiency. Metastatic calcification is
associated with hypercalcemia, which may be caused by vitamin D intoxication rather than
deficiency.
Pathologic calcification is the abnormal tissue deposition of calcium salts, together with smaller
amounts of iron, magnesium, and other mineral salts. It is of two types namely metastatic and
dystrophic calcification.
Metastatic calcification is the deposition of calcium salts in otherwise normal tissues.
• Hypercalcemia secondary to some disturbance in calcium metabolism.
• Principal causes of hypercalcemia:
• Increased secretion of parathyroid hormone (PTH)
• Resorption of bone tissue
• Vitamin-D-related disorders- excess or abnormal sensitivity
• Renal failure
• Aluminum intoxication
• Milk-alkali syndrome- due to excess ingestion of calcium
Metastatic calcification can be seen in interstitial tissues of the gastric mucosa, kidneys, lungs,
systemic arteries, and pulmonary veins.

subject: Pathology
question: Match the cytokines with the effector cells .
explanation:
The correct answer is option B:
Cytokines are proteins produced by many cell types (principally activated lymphocytes,
macrophages, and dendritic cells, but also endothelial, epithelial, and connective tissue cells) that
mediate and regulate immune and inflammatory reactions.
Three subsets of CD4+ T cells secrete different types of cytokines and elicit different types of
inflammation.
TH1 cells - activate macrophages by the classical pathway by secreting IFNγ.
TH2 cells - recruit and activate eosinophils and are responsible for the alternative pathway of
macrophage activation by secreting IL4, IL5, and IL13.
• IL4 acts on B cells to stimulate class switching to IgE and promotes the development of
additional TH2 cells.
• IL5 is involved in the development and activation of eosinophils, which are essential effectors of
type I hypersensitivity
2162
(discussed later).
• IL13 enhances IgE production and acts on epithelial cells to stimulate mucus secretion. In
addition, TH2 cells (as well as mast cells and epithelial cells) produce chemokines that attract
more TH2 cells, as well as other leukocytes, to the reaction site. It also shares some functional
similarities to IL-4 and can thus induce differentiation in dendritic cells.
TH17 cells secrete IL17 and other cytokines responsible
for neutrophils and monocyte recruitment.
1. IL
4
C. B cells
2. IL
5
A. Eosinophils
3.IL9
B. Mast cells
4. IL
13
D. Dendritic cells

subject: Pathology
question:
2100
The following is a histopathological image of thyroid pathology. What is the diagnosis?

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-45-image]
explanation:
The histopathological image shows the presence of amyloid-rich stroma (pinkish material) and
nests of polygonal cells, which are findings suggestive of medullary carcinoma of thyroid.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-45-exp-image]
Medullary carcinoma of the thyroid (MTC) arises from the parafollicular C cells. Most of the MTC
are sporadic, but they may also be associated with inheritable syndromes like familial
MTC, MEN2A, and MEN2B. All the inherited variants are secondary to RET gene mutations.
Most patients present at 50–60 years and have a female preponderance. MTC secretes calcitonin,
carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), prostaglandins E2 and F2α, histaminase, serotonin,
and adrenocorticotrophic hormone (ACTH).
2163
Features of MTC
• Painful neck mass with cervical lymphadenopathy
• Dysphagia, dysphonia, or dyspnea if there is local invasion
• Diarrhea due to increased intestinal motility
• Cushingoid habitus due to ectopic ACTH production
• Blood-borne metastasis
• Microscopically, they consist of sheets of neoplastic cells separated by collagen and amyloid
stroma
Diagnosis is by careful family history, physical examination, raised calcitonin and CEA levels, and
fine-needle aspiration biopsy of the mass.
Other options:
Option B: The image below shows Orphan Annie-eye nuclei with central clearing and margination
of chromatin in papillary carcinoma of the thyroid.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-45-exp-image]
Option C: Follicular carcinoma thyroid on histopathology may show uniform cells forming small
follicles containing colloid (resembling normal thyroid gland) or sheets of cells without colloid.
2164

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-45-exp-image]
Option D: Anaplastic carcinomas are poorly differentiated thyroid carcinomas with varied
morphology, including giant cells or spindle-shaped cells or both. There is minimal follicular
differentiation with intratumoral necrosis.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-45-exp-image]

subject: Pathology
question:
The Hb is 5g/dL and the absolute reticulocyte count percentage is 9%. What is the corrected
reticulocyte count?
explanation:
The corrected reticulocyte count (CRC) of this patient is 3.
CRC = Reticulocyte %  x  (Actual Hb / Normal Hb)
According to the values given in this question:
CRC = 9 x 5/15 = 3
Alternatively, CRC can be calculated using hematocrit (PCV) as follows:
CRC = Reticulocyte %  x  (Actual PCV / Normal PCV)
Note: With these calculations, take normal Hb as 15g/dl and normal PCV as 45%.

subject: Pathology
question:
A 7-year-old child presents with fever, erythematous rash, and bone pain. X-ray of the skull
shows multiple lytic lesions. Skin biopsy shows prominent nuclear grooves with eosinophils
as in the image below. Immunohistochemistry for S100, CD1a is also shown in the images
below. What would be the diagnosis?

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-48-image]
explanation:
The above clinical scenario along with the histopathological image showing Langerhans cells with
folded nuclei (image A), CD1a (image B), and positive S100 (image C), is suggestive of
Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH).
LCH occurs due to the proliferation of a special type of immature dendritic cells,
called Langerhans cells. Their normal function is to present antigens. The most common mutation
is the BRAFV600E gain-of-function mutation.
It is characterized by single or multiple osteolytic bone lesions and can also infiltrate nearly every
organ, like the skin (of the scalp), lungs, liver, spleen, bone marrow, or the central nervous
system.
LCH can present as the following clinical entities:
• Multifocal multisystem Langerhans cell histiocytosis (Letterer-Siwe disease) - mostly seen in
infants
2166
• Unifocal and multifocal unisystem Langerhans cell histiocytosis (eosinophilic granuloma) - The
combination of bone defects, diabetes insipidus, and exophthalmos is called
the Hand-Schüller-Christian triad - mostly seen in younger children
• Pulmonary LCH - mostly seen in adults
Clinical features include pain and swelling in the affected area. When calvarium is affected, it
results in otitis media and mastoiditis. Spinal involvement results in compression fractures and
neurological involvement. Vertebral involvement results in a rapid flattening of the vertebral body
(vertebra plana).
Histopathologically, Langerhans cells have a single large nucleus with a characteristic folded or
grooved nucleus (coffee-bean appearance) with small inconspicuous nucleoli. Birbeck granules are
seen in the cytoplasm. The tumor cells specifically express HLA-DR, S-100, and
CD1a and langerin (CD207) markers.
The image below shows a histopathological image of Langerhans cells with folded nuclei.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-48-exp-image]
The presence of Birbeck granules in the cytoplasm is a characteristic finding of Langerhans cell
histiocytosis (LCH) on electron microscopy.
Birbeck granules have a dilated terminal end producing a tennis racket–like appearance.
It contains the protein langerin (CD207). It is seen on electron microscopy. The image below
shows Birbeck granules on electron microscopy seen in LCH.
2167

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-48-exp-image]
The below image shows lytic lesion of skull seen in LCH.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-48-exp-image]
Option A: Rosai-Dorfman syndrome is otherwise called sinus histiocytosis with massive
lymphadenopathy. Rosai-Dorfman disease is not a histiocytic neoplasm. It is an idiopathic disease
characterized by Painless lymphadenopathy, usually of cervical nodes. The disorder
predominantly affects children, adolescents, and young adults. It may present with nonspecific
symptoms and management mostly involves observation rather than specific treatment.
Option B: Kimura disease is s a chronic inflammatory disorder of unknown etiology that most
commonly presents as painless lymphadenopathy.
Option D: Juvenile xanthogranuloma is a non-Langerhans cell histiocytosis occurring in early
infancy and childhood. It presents as reddish-brown papular lesions, which evolve to become
orange. The lesions are most frequently seen in the face, scalp, and upper torso. They may be
single or multiple lesions. Single lesions are the most common. The characteristic multinucleated
giant cell, Touton cell is observed in 85% of juvenile xanthogranulomas.
2168

subject: Pathology
question: Match the following cell types with their associated malignancy.
explanation:
The correct matches are as follows:
Popcorn cell is a lymphohistiocytic (L&H) variant of Reed Steenberg (RS) cells that are
characteristic of lymphocyte-predominant Hodgkin lymphoma. It has a delicate multilobed, puffy
nucleus resembling popped corn. The image below shows a popcorn cell seen in lymphocytic
predominant Hodgkin's lymphoma.
1. Popcorn cell
b. Lymphocyte-predominant 
Hodgkin's lymphoma
2. Faggot cell
a. Acute promyelocytic leuke
mia
3. Cerebriform nucle
i
d. Sezary syndrome
4. Starry sky
c. Burkitt lymphoma

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-49-exp-image]
Multiple Auer rods clustered together within a single cell resembling a bundle of sticks are known
as faggot cells and are highly suggestive of acute promyelocytic leukemia. Histopathology from
bone marrow aspirate reveals numerous Auer rods (needle-like azurophilic granules) in the
cytoplasm of the promyelocytes. Faggot cells are shown below.
2169

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-49-exp-image]
Sézary-Lutzner cells - cells with cerebriform nuclei (infolded/hyper-convoluted nuclear
membrane).
The histological hallmark of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma (mycosis fungoides type) is the presence
of Sezary-Lutzner cells, which form band-like aggregates within the superficial dermis and invade
the epidermis as single cells and small clusters called Pautrier microabscess (marked by
arrow). These cells have markedly infolded nuclear membranes giving them a cerebriform contour
as seen below.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-49-exp-image]
Microscopy in Burkitt lymphoma shows a characteristic starry sky pattern. The neoplastic cells
are homogeneous, medium-sized B cells with frequent mitotic figures and reactive
macrophages scattered through the tumor, with their pale cytoplasm in a background of
blue-staining tumor cells giving the tumor a so-called "starry sky" appearance.
The image given below shows a starry sky pattern seen in Burkitt lymphoma.
2170

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-49-exp-image]

subject: Pathology
question:
2102
Hereditary angioedema is due to a defect in which of the proteins mentioned below?
explanation:
Hereditary angioedema is an autosomal dominant condition characterized by a deficiency of C1
esterase inhibitors. C1 esterase inhibitors usually target proteases like C1r and C1s, factor XII and
the kallikrein system. Therefore, their deficiency leads to unregulated activation of kallikrein,
leading to increased bradykinin.
Clinical features include swelling of the skin and mucosal surfaces, nausea, vomiting, and
diarrhea, which may be aggravated after minor trauma or emotional stress. The condition can
be treated by giving C1 inhibitor concentrates from human plasma.
The main inflammatory mediator responsible for these symptoms is bradykinin. Bradykinin is an
inflammatory mediator formed by the cleavage of kininogen, in the presence of the enzyme
kallikrein. Bradykinin has the following effects:
• Increases the vascular permeability
• Causes contraction of smooth muscle
• Dilates the blood vessel
• Mediates pain
Other options:
Options B and C: Decay-accelerating factor (DAF) and CD59 are two proteins that are linked by a
glycophosphatidylinositol (GPI) anchor to the plasma membrane. DAF prevents the formation of
C3 convertases and CD59 inhibits the formation of the MAC. An acquired deficiency of the
enzyme that creates GPI anchors leads to a deficiency of these regulators and excessive
complement activation and lysis of red cells resulting in Paroxysmal nocturnal
hemoglobinuria(PNH).
During inflammation, C5a (Option D), C3a, and C4a, which are all cleavage products of their
corresponding complement components, stimulate mast cells to release histamine. They have
effects similar to those of mast cell mediators involved in anaphylactic reactions and therefore, are
2171
called anaphylatoxins. C5a is also chemotactic for neutrophils, monocytes, eosinophils, and
basophils. Additionally, it activates the lipooxygenase pathway of arachidonic acid metabolism in
neutrophils and monocytes, resulting in the further release of inflammatory mediators.

subject: Pathology
question:
A 35-year-old woman presents with fatigue. Investigations revealed the following: Hb, 5 gm
%; MCH, 24; low MCV; leukocytes, 11,000/uL, and platelets, 5 lakhs. The peripheral smear is
shown below. What is the diagnosis?

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-51-image]
explanation:
The given scenario of a 35 year old woman, her iron profile and peripheral smear showing
microcytic hypochromic anemia are suggestive of iron-deficiency anemia (IDA).
IDA is an example of microcytic hypochromic anemia. The causes of IDA can be inadequate diet,
impaired reabsorption, increased requirement and increased blood loss.
The manifestations of IDA depend upon the underlying cause. In general, IDA presents with
koilonychia, alopecia, atrophic changes in the tongue, gastric mucosa, malabsorbtion and pica
(craving for consumption of non-edible materials)
Peripheral smears show small and pale red cells typically described as microcytic (reduced MCV)
and hypochromic (enlarged zone of central pallor). Poikilocytosis can be seen in the form of
elongated red cells, known as pencil cells. Target cells can be found in cases of severe IDA.
Diagnosis is mainly by laboratory studies. The typical findings are:
• Low hemoglobin and hematocrit
• Low serum iron and ferretin
• Increased Total Iron Binding Capacity (TIBC)
• Hepcidin is low (decreased stores of iron inhibit hepcidin synthesis)
IDA is treated by giving oral and parenteral preparations if iron or packed cell transfusion
depending upon the severity of the anemia.
Option A: Megaloblastic anemia is defined by a high MCV
Option C: Thalassemia presents much earlier in infancy, with reduced MCV and a
normal/increased RBC count
Option D: Essential thrombocytosis is characterized by isolated rise in platelet count
(>450,000) along with normal hemoglobin, hematocrit and iron stores.

subject: Pathology
question: Which of the following statements is true about interleukin-12?
explanation:
The true statements are that interleukin-12 (IL-12) is produced by macrophages and increases the
production of interferon-γ (IFN-γ).
IL-12 is produced by dendritic cells and macrophages. Its principle actions are to promote the
production of IFN-γ, which drives TH1-type responses and further stimulates macrophage
activation. It also promotes the differentiation of CD4+ T-cells to Th1 cells.
2172

subject: Pathology
question:
A chronic smoker presents to the clinic with shortness of breath. A mass is noted at the lower
lobe periphery of the lung. A diagnosis of lung adenocarcinoma is made. The biopsy will be
positive for:
explanation:
The biopsy of adenocarcinoma of the lung will be positive for thyroid transcription
factor-1 (TTF-1) and napsin A.
Adenocarcinoma is an invasive malignant epithelial tumor with glandular differentiation or mucin
production by the tumor cells. It is associated with tobacco smoking, but less so than other
histologic subtypes. It is the most common subtype in non-smokers and is associated with the loss
of function of EGFR, ALK, ROS1, MET or RET genes.
The majority express TTF-1, which is a protein required for normal lung development. Napsin A is
a new marker and is more sensitive and specific than TTF-1 in the diagnosis of adenocarcinoma.
Adenocarcinomas grow in various patterns: acinar, lepidic, papillary, micropapillary, and solid.
The lesions are small and are usually located in the periphery.  At the periphery of the tumor there
is often a lepidic pattern of spread, in which the tumor cells “crawl” along normal-appearing
alveolar septa.
Clinical features usually have an insidious onset. The incidence is usually at age 50 or more. The
major presenting complaints are cough, weight loss, chest pain, and dyspnea.
Other options:
Option B: p40, along with p63 is a marker for squamous cell carcinoma of the lung.
Option D: Chromogranin is a marker for small cell carcinoma of the lung.

subject: Pathology
question:
The following image shows an instrument used to estimate hemoglobin levels. What does it
measure?

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-54-image]
explanation:
The instrument in the image is Sahli's hemoglobinometer and is used to measure acid hematin
levels of a given blood sample.
In Sahli's method, oxyhemoglobin is converted to acid hematin with the action of dilute
hydrochloric acid (N/10 HCl). The brown color of the acid hematin is matched to its intensity with
a standard brown glass comparator in a visual colorimeter called Sahli’s colorimeter.
Sahli's method is a simple bedside test and is cost-effective. However, it has some disadvantages
which include visual errors, inability to measure other forms of hemoglobin, fading of the
comparator over time, and the color of acid hematin fading quickly.

subject: Pathology
question:
2104
Iron in tissues is stained by:
explanation:
Perl's Prussian blue stain is used in histopathological studies to detect the presence of iron in the
specimen. Iron is stained blue with this stain.
It is used in hereditary hemochromatosis to stain liver tissue, which shows hepatocellular iron. It
also stains the iron-laden mitochondria of ring sideroblasts seen in lead
toxicity and myelodysplastic syndrome
The following image shows the blue staining of iron (in the form of hemosiderin) deposited in
hepatocytes:
2173

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-55-exp-image]
Other options:
Option A: Masson's trichrome stain is used to stain collagen.
Option C: PAS stain (Periodic acid Schiff stain) is used for the detection of glycogen in tissues such
as the liver, skeletal muscle, etc., and also in tumor cells.
Option D: Fite-Faraco stain has been used in cases of leprosy for staining Mycobacterium
leprae. It can also be used to stain Nocardia.
The image below shows acid-fast bacilli inside foamy macrophages:

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-55-exp-image]

subject: Pathology
question:
A woman presents with lesions on the inner thighs and peri-anal region. They are nodular,
4-6 mm in size, and appear pale. The histopathological image shows multiple
intracytoplasmic inclusion bodies consistent with Henderson-Patterson bodies. The diagnosis
is:

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-56-image]
explanation:
The diagnosis based on the given clinical scenario and the histological image is molluscum
contagiosum, which shows intracytoplasmic inclusion bodies, called Henderson–Patterson
2174
bodies or molluscum bodies.
Infection is usually spread by direct contact, particularly among children and young adults.
Multiple lesions occur on the skin and mucous membranes, with a predilection for the trunk and
anogenital areas. Individual lesions are firm, pruritic, pink to skin-colored umbilicated papules. A
curd-like material can be expressed from the central umbilication.
Smearing this material onto a glass slide followed by Giemsa staining shows diagnostic
molluscum bodies, which are ellipsoid, homogeneous, cytoplasmic inclusion bodies that are found
in the cells of the stratum granulosum and the stratum corneum and are up to 35 µm in size.
The image below shows umbilicated papules of molluscum contagiosum.

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-56-exp-image]

[Pathology-INI-CET-2022-56-exp-image]
Other options:
Option A: Trichodysplasia spinulosa is a condition characterized by painless erythematous
papules on the face with folliculocentric keratin spines. It is caused by trichodysplasia
spinulosa-associated polyomavirus. Histologic features are keratin plugging, abundant dystrophic
2175
and cornfield inner root sheath epithelium, and numerous, large eosinophilic trichohyalin
granules.
Option B: Koilocytes are characteristically seen in HPV infections, such as condylomata
acuminata. Koilocytes are lining cells which have perinuclear cytoplasmic vacuolization.
Option D: Donovan bodies are seen in donovanosis, caused by Klebsiella granulomatis. Donovan
bodies are minute, encapsulated coccobacilli in macrophages, which can be demonstrated in
Giemsa-stained smears of the exudate.

subject: Pathology
question: Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction is caused by all of the following cytokines except:
explanation:
INF-γ is not involved in Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction (JHR).
JHR is a condition that occurs in patients infected by spirochetes who undergo antibiotic
treatment. The reaction occurs within 24 hours of antibiotic treatment. Clinical features are fever,
chills, nausea and vomiting, tachycardia, hypotension, flushing, myalgia, and exacerbation of skin
lesions.
Syphilis is the leading spirochetal infection associated with JHR. Other infections are Lyme
disease, louse-borne relapsing fever, leptospirosis, and tick-borne relapsing fever. Antibiotics that
have been most associated are penicillins, tetracyclines, and erythromycin.
Mild reactions are self-limiting and often resolve spontaneously within 24 hours. Prevention of
the JHR is by giving TNF-alpha antibodies and in some cases steroids.
2176

subject: Pathology
question: Choose the correct statement regarding the telomerase theory of aging.

options.a: Telomere stability is associated with aging
options.b: Abnormal telomerase activation is associated with aging
options.c: Decreased telomere length is associated with aging
options.d: Increased telomere length is associated with aging

explanation:
A decreased telomere length is associated with aging.
Telomeres are short TG-rich repeated sequences of DNA present at the ends of linear eukaryotic
chromosomes. Telomeres do not code for any proteins. They are important for ensuring the
complete replication of chromosome ends and for protecting the ends from fusion and
degradation.
Normally, with every cell division, there is some shortening of telomeres at the ends of
chromosomes. Once the telomeres are shortened beyond a certain point, it may cause cell-cycle
arrest or apoptosis. This is called replicative senescence.
Telomerase is the enzyme that prevents the shortening of telomeres. It is a specialized
RNA-protein complex that uses its own RNA as a template for adding nucleotides to the ends of
chromosomes.
This enzyme is usually present in germ cells but is absent in most somatic cells. Therefore most of
the somatic cells age, their telomeres become shorter and they exit the cell cycle, resulting in an
inability to generate new cells to replace damaged cells. Abnormal telomerase reactivation in
somatic cells leads to evasion of senescence and apoptosis. It enables the cells to divide several
times than usual.
In stem cells and cancer cells, telomerase is usually reactivated and telomere length is stabilized,
allowing the cells to proliferate indefinitely.

subject: Pathology
question:
A child presents with recurrent chest infections and abdominal pain. There is a history of 1
blood transfusion in the past. On examination, he had icterus and mild splenomegaly.
Electrophoresis shows increased HbA2, HbF, and S spike. What is the likely diagnosis?
explanation:
The given scenario with electrophoresis showing increased HbA2, HbF, and S spike is suggestive
of sickle cell disease.
Sickle cell anemia is an autosomal recessive disorder that occurs due to the point mutation in the
β-globin gene which results in the replacement of glutamate residue with valine. This causes the
production of sickle-shaped red blood cells due to the polymerization of deoxygenated hemoglobin
(HbS).
Factors that predispose to sickling are infection, dehydration, and acidosis.
The clinical features of sickle cell disease are due to the tendency of HbS molecules to stack into
polymers when deoxygenated. The features are:
• Moderately severe hemolytic anemia with jaundice.
• Painful bone crisis- hand-foot syndrome or dactylitis of the bones of hands and feet.
• Acute chest syndrome typically presents with fever, cough, chest pain, and pulmonary infiltrates.
• Hyposthenuria- inability to concentrate urine.
2181
• Aplastic crisis stems from the infection of red cell progenitors by parvovirus B19, which causes a
transient cessation of erythropoiesis and a sudden worsening of the anemia.
• Spleen
• In children- splenic sequestration crises occur which results in splenomegaly due to red pulp
congestion.
• Adolescence or early adulthood- autosplenectomy occurs due to splenic infarction, fibrosis, and
progressive shrinkage.
• Increased susceptibility to infection with encapsulated organisms like pneumococcal,
meningococcal, and Haemophilus influenza septicemia, but can be reduced by vaccination and
prophylactic antibiotics.
• Other clinical features- priapism, leg ulcers, stroke, retinopathy, pigment gallstones, and growth
retardation.
The diagnosis is suggested by the clinical findings, the presence of irreversibly sickled red cells on
peripheral smear, and hemoglobin electrophoresis showing increased levels of HbA2, HbF, and S
spike. The changes in the skull resemble a crewcut in radiographic studies as a result of
compensatory erythroid hyperplasia.
The image given below shows the sickling of RBCs in sickle cell disease.

[Pathology-NEET-2022-2-exp-image]
The image given below shows Howell-Jolly bodies seen in asplenia.
2182

[Pathology-NEET-2022-2-exp-image]
Note: The sickle cell trait protects against falciparum malaria in certain African populations.
Other options:
Option A: Beta-thalassemia is an autosomal codominant disorder, caused by mutations in alpha or
beta globin that reduce hemoglobin synthesis. Peripheral smear shows microcytic, hypochromic
cells with anisocytosis, poikilocytosis, target cells, and basophilic stippling. Hemoglobin
electrophoresis shows increased HbF and HbA2.
Option B: HbC disease is caused by mutation in the beta-globin chain in which glutamate is
replaced by lysine in the sixth position of the beta-globin chain. Patients usually present with very
mild anemia and microcytosis. Peripheral smear shows hexagonal HbC crystals. Hemoglobin
electrophoresis shows increased HbC levels.
Option D: Acute coronary syndrome is very rare in children and may be associated with Kawasaki
disease. Children with Kawasaki disease present with fever, conjunctival ingestion, strawberry
tongue, polymorphous rash, cervical lymphadenopathy, and hand and foot changes. They are
treated with intravenous immunoglobulin and aspirin.

subject: Pathology
question: Why do neoplastic cells utilize Warburg metabolism?

options.a: It decreases glucose utilisation by neoplastic cells
options.b: It forms metabolic intermediates which are needed for cell growth and multiplication
options.c: It provides more energy in the form of increased ATP production
options.d: It prevents apoptosis and makes the cancer cells immortal

explanation:
Neoplastic cells utilize Warburg metabolism for the generation of metabolic intermediates, which
are needed for cell growth and multiplication.
The Warburg Effect is a phenomenon in which there is an increase in the rate of glucose uptake
and conversion into lactate(fermentation), even in the presence of oxygen. Cancer cells derive
energy mainly from glycolysis.
Cancer cells utilize glycolysis (2 ATP) over mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation (36 ATP) for
glucose metabolism, even though the latter produces more ATP. It is because pure oxidative
phosphorylation produces abundant ATP, but also burns glucose to CO2 and H2O, leaving no
carbon moieties suitable for use as building blocks for lipids or proteins.
2183
For this reason, rapidly growing cells, by undergoing glycolysis, produce carbon moieties that are
needed for growth and cellular maintenance.
The image given below shows the comparison of glucose metabolism in normal cells versus cancer
cells.

[Pathology-NEET-2022-3-exp-image]
As a result of decreased ATP production, the main metabolic function of mitochondria in growing
cells is not to generate ATP, but to carry
out reactions that generate cellular building blocks. For example, lipid biosynthesis.
Warburg effect is produced by growth factor receptors downstream signalling pathways. These
include receptor tyrosine kinase/PI3K/AKT and MYC.
Clinically, cancer cells exhibiting the Warburg effect show a property of glucose hunger. This is
used to visualize them via positron emission tomography (PET) scanning.

subject: Pathology
question:
An elderly patient presents with anemia and hemoglobinuria. Investigations reveal increased
lactate dehydrogenase (LDH). The peripheral smear image is given below. Which of the
following physical examination findings can support the likely diagnosis?
2177

[Pathology-NEET-2022-4-image]
explanation:
The above scenario with anemia, increased LDH, and hemoglobinuria along with peripheral
smear showing schistocytes are suggestive of macroangiopathic hemolytic anemia (MAHA). The
presence of a mechanical second heart sound can support the diagnosis.
Macroangiopathic hemolytic anemia is a type of hemolytic anemia that results from trauma to red
cells by artificial mechanical cardiac valves that produce turbulent blood flow and pressure
gradients that cause hemolysis from shear forces. This traumatic damage leads to intravascular
hemolysis and the appearance of red cell fragments (schistocytes), burr cells, helmet cells, and
triangle cells in blood smears.
The given below peripheral smear shows schistocytes and helmet cells.
2184

[Pathology-NEET-2022-4-exp-image]
Laboratory findings in hemolytic anemia resulting from trauma to red cells:
• Hemoglobin- decreased
• Reticulocytes- increased
• Platelets- normal
• Unconjugated bilirubin- increased
• Lactate dehydrogenase- increased
• Haptoglobin- decreased
• Hemoglobinuria due to intravascular hemolysis
Treatment is conservative. But, if more than mild anemia develops, reintervention to correct
regurgitation may be required.
Option A: Splenomegaly is seen in various disorders of white and red cells. But splenomegaly in
intravascular hemolysis is less likely.
Option B: Frontal bossing is seen in thalassemia due to expansion of hematopoietically active
marrow. An X-ray of the skull shows a 'crewcut' appearance. Peripheral smear in thalassemia
shows microcytic hypochromic cells with anisocytosis, poikilocytosis, and target cells.
Option D: Goiter is usually associated with iron deficiency anemia. A peripheral smear shows
microcytic hypochromic cells.

subject: Pathology
question:
All of the following statements are true regarding neutrophil extracellular trapping (NET)
except that

options.a: It is detected in blood during sepsis.
options.b: It is produced in response to bacterial infection.
options.d: It is chromatin with antibacterial enzymes.

explanation:
Mitochondrial DNA is not seen in neutrophil extracellular traps (NET).
Neutrophilic extracellular traps are networks of extracellular fibers that provide a high
concentration of antimicrobial substances at the site of infection and prevent the spread of the
microbes by trapping them in the fibrils.
2185
Neutrophilic extracellular traps (NET) are the meshwork of nuclear chromatin of neutrophils. The
loss of nuclei leads to the death of the neutrophils. They concentrate antimicrobial peptides and
enzymes to fight against bacteria and fungus (not viruses). They are detected in the blood during
sepsis. They are produced by neutrophils in response to:
• Infectious pathogens: mainly bacteria and fungi
• Inflammatory mediators: chemokines, cytokines (mainly interferons), complement proteins, and
reactive oxygen species (ROS)
Note: The nuclear chromatin in the NETs, which includes histones and associated DNA, has been
postulated to be a source of nuclear antigens in systemic autoimmune diseases, particularly
systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE).

subject: Pathology
question:
